results.original_text: Bulbus Allii Cepae — the dried or fresh bulb of Allium cepa L. (Alliaceae/Liliaceae s.l.). Key chemical constituents include: flavonoids (quercetin and its glycosides, principally quercetin-4'-glucoside and quercetin-3,4'-diglucoside, comprising up to 10% of dry weight in outer scales); organosulfur compounds (S-alk(en)yl-L-cysteine sulfoxides, principally S-(1-propenyl)-L-cysteine sulfoxide [isoalliin], S-propyl-L-cysteine sulfoxide, and S-methyl-L-cysteine sulfoxide; allicin-related thiosulfinates formed enzymatically by alliinase upon tissue disruption); phenolic acids (caffeic acid, sinapic acid, protocatechuic acid); sterols (beta-sitosterol); saponins; and fructooligosaccharides (fructans, comprising up to 25–40% of fresh weight). Volatile fraction includes dipropyl disulfide, propanethial S-oxide (the lachrymatory factor), and related sulfides.
results.translation: Onion (Allium cepa) bulb has clinically and pharmacologically supported uses primarily as an adjunct treatment for prevention of atherosclerosis and management of dyslipidaemia, and for symptomatic relief of appetite loss. Its principal pharmacological mechanisms include inhibition of platelet aggregation (attributed to organosulfur thiosulfinates and ajoene-related compounds), antioxidant activity (attributed predominantly to quercetin glycosides, which scavenge reactive oxygen species and inhibit LDL oxidation), and modest lipid-lowering effects demonstrated in controlled trials. Additional pharmacological activities documented in vitro and in animal studies include antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory (inhibition of cyclooxygenase and lipoxygenase pathways), hypoglycaemic, and fibrinolytic effects. Traditional and pharmacopoeial uses extend to treatment of coughs, colds, bronchitis, and febrile conditions (preparations of fresh juice or syrup), as well as topical application for insect stings, minor burns, and wound healing. The lachrymatory factor (propanethial S-oxide) and thiosulfinates are produced enzymatically by alliinase action on cysteine sulfoxide precursors only upon mechanical disruption of the tissue, meaning preparation method significantly affects the active constituent profile delivered.
results.preparation: WHO-supported adult dosage forms and doses: (1) Fresh bulb (raw or lightly cooked): 50 g daily. (2) Dried bulb powder: 4–20 g per day in divided doses. (3) Fresh juice: 50 mL daily (approximately equivalent to one medium bulb). (4) Tincture (1:5 in 45% ethanol): 15–40 mL per day in divided doses. (5) Onion syrup (traditional cough preparation): prepared by macerating fresh sliced bulb in honey or sugar syrup; dose 1 tablespoon (approximately 15 mL) 3–4 times daily for respiratory indications. Standardised extracts should be standardised to quercetin or thiosulfinate content where available. No paediatric WHO dosage formally established; paediatric use follows traditional practice with dose reduction by body weight.
results.condition: Atherosclerosis prevention; dyslipidaemia; platelet aggregation inhibition; appetite loss (anorexia); chronic bronchitis and upper respiratory catarrh (traditional/supportive); hypertension (adjunctive, traditional evidence)
results.source_section: WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 1 — Bulbus Allii Cepae

entities_mentioned.name: Allium cepa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cultivated onion, family Amaryllidaceae (formerly Alliaceae/Liliaceae s.l.)
entities_mentioned.notes: Widely cultivated globally; native range believed to be Central Asia (Iran, Pakistan, Afghanistan). The WHO monograph covers the bulb (Bulbus Allii Cepae) as the official plant part.

entities_mentioned.name: Quercetin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Flavonol aglycone; CAS 117-39-5
entities_mentioned.notes: Major antioxidant flavonoid in onion outer scales; present primarily as glucosides in the flesh. Responsible for much of the antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Isoalliin (S-(1-propenyl)-L-cysteine sulfoxide)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Principal cysteine sulfoxide precursor in Allium cepa; distinct from alliin (the principal precursor in Allium sativum)
entities_mentioned.notes: Converted by alliinase upon cell disruption to thiosulfinates including propanethial S-oxide (lachrymatory factor) and 1-propenyl thiosulfinates. Profile differs substantially from garlic.

entities_mentioned.name: Alliinase
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: C-S lyase enzyme (EC 4.4.1.4); present in all Allium species
entities_mentioned.notes: Compartmentalised from substrates in intact tissue; released and activated by mechanical disruption (cutting, crushing). Heat inactivates the enzyme, altering the therapeutic constituent profile of cooked versus raw preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Propanethial S-oxide
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The lachrymatory factor of onion; volatile sulfenic acid derivative
entities_mentioned.notes: Responsible for the characteristic eye-irritating effect of cut onions. Rapidly volatilises; reduced or absent in cooked or dried preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Fructooligosaccharides / Fructans
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Inulin-type fructans; prebiotic dietary fibre fraction
entities_mentioned.notes: Comprise a substantial portion of onion fresh weight. Contribute to prebiotic and potential glycaemic-modulating effects but are not the primary focus of the WHO monograph indication.

results.original_text: Bulbus Allii Sativi (Allium sativum L., Fam. Alliaceae/Liliaceae). Principal active constituents: alliin (S-allyl-L-cysteine sulfoxide, ~0.5–1.3% fresh weight); allicin (diallyl thiosulfinate, formed enzymatically from alliin via alliinase upon crushing; ~0.1–0.4% fresh weight); γ-glutamyl-S-allylcysteine and γ-glutamyl-S-trans-1-propenylcysteine (major storage compounds, ~0.9% fresh weight); diallyl disulfide (DADS), diallyl trisulfide (DATS), allyl methyl disulfide (volatile organosulfur compounds present in steam distillate); ajoene (E- and Z-isomers, formed in oil macerates); fructans (fructooligosaccharides, ~16% dry weight); adenosine; flavonoids (quercetin glycosides); saponins; selenium compounds.
results.translation:
Garlic (the dried or fresh bulb of Allium sativum) has been used medicinally for thousands of years and is one of the most extensively studied medicinal plants in the WHO formulary. Its principal pharmacological activity derives from a cascade of organosulfur compounds: the intact bulb contains the odourless precursor alliin, which is converted by the enzyme alliinase to the unstable, pungent compound allicin immediately upon tissue disruption (crushing or chopping). Allicin itself rapidly degrades into a family of secondary thiosulfinates, disulfides, and trisulfides, as well as the distinctive compound ajoene in oil-based preparations. Together, these compounds account for garlic's clinically documented cardiovascular, antimicrobial, and antineoplastic [cancer-preventive] properties.

CARDIOVASCULAR EFFECTS: The strongest clinical evidence supports garlic's use as an adjunct in the management of hyperlipidaemia [elevated blood lipids] and mild hypertension [elevated blood pressure]. Meta-analyses of randomised controlled trials (RCTs) demonstrate that standardised garlic preparations (standardised to 1.3% alliin or 0.6% allicin yield) reduce total serum cholesterol by approximately 4–12% and LDL-cholesterol by 4–9% relative to placebo over 8–24 weeks of treatment. Garlic also produces modest but statistically significant reductions in systolic blood pressure (approximately 7–8 mmHg) and diastolic blood pressure (approximately 4–5 mmHg) in patients with mild-to-moderate hypertension. These effects are mediated in part by inhibition of HMG-CoA reductase [the rate-limiting enzyme in cholesterol synthesis], inhibition of platelet aggregation (via suppression of thromboxane B2 synthesis and cyclic AMP phosphodiesterase), and promotion of endogenous fibrinolysis [breakdown of blood clots]. Ajoene and DATS have been shown in vitro to inhibit platelet aggregation synergistically with prostaglandin inhibitors.

ANTIMICROBIAL EFFECTS: Allicin and its sulphide derivatives exhibit broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity in vitro against gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria (including Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli, Salmonella spp., and Helicobacter pylori), dermatophytic fungi (including Candida albicans and Trichophyton spp.), and certain parasites. The mechanism involves allicin's direct reaction with thiol-containing enzymes (particularly thiol-dependent proteases and dehydrogenases) in microbial cells, disrupting their metabolism. In vivo clinical evidence for antimicrobial effects is limited but includes preliminary trial data supporting topical ajoene for treatment of tinea pedis [athlete's foot] and tinea corporis [ringworm].

ANTINEOPLASTIC / CHEMOPREVENTIVE EFFECTS: Epidemiological data and animal model studies suggest that regular garlic consumption is associated with reduced incidence of gastric and colorectal cancers. DATS and ajoene have demonstrated pro-apoptotic [cell-death-promoting] and anti-proliferative effects in multiple human cancer cell lines in vitro. While these findings are encouraging, WHO monograph guidance notes that evidence from controlled human trials is insufficient to recommend garlic as a primary anti-cancer treatment.

TRADITIONAL USES: Garlic has been described in the Ayurvedic, Chinese, Egyptian, Greek (Hippocratic and Galenic), and European folk medicine traditions for treatment of respiratory infections (common cold, bronchitis), intestinal parasites, dysentery, skin infections, and as a general tonic and immune stimulant. Pharmacopoeial listings include the European Pharmacopoeia, British Pharmacopoeia, and several national pharmacopoeias of WHO Member States.

ADVERSE REACTIONS: The most common adverse effects are gastrointestinal (nausea, flatulence, diarrhoea, heartburn), particularly with raw garlic or high-dose preparations. Characteristic halitosis [garlic breath] and body odour are dose-dependent. Allergic contact dermatitis has been reported with topical use, and occupational asthma has been documented in garlic-processing workers. There is clinically documented potentiation of anticoagulant drugs (warfarin, aspirin, clopidogrel); patients on anticoagulation therapy should use garlic preparations only under medical supervision.

CONTRAINDICATIONS: Known hypersensitivity to garlic or other Allium species. High-dose medicinal garlic should be avoided in the perioperative period [around surgery] due to platelet-inhibitory effects. Use with caution during pregnancy (emmenagogue [menstruation-stimulating] effects at high doses) and breastfeeding (may alter milk flavour; rare reports of infant colic).

entities_mentioned.name: Allium sativum
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Garlic — Allium sativum L. (Family Alliaceae, formerly Liliaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Universally cultivated geophyte native to Central Asia (likely Kyrgyzstan/Tajikistan region); now cultivated globally. The medicinal part is the dried or fresh compound bulb (Bulbus Allii Sativi). Taxonomic placement in Alliaceae (as a segregate of Liliaceae) is now standard per APG IV classification.

entities_mentioned.name: Alliin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: S-allyl-L-cysteine sulfoxide (IUPAC: (2R)-2-amino-3-[(S)-prop-2-enylsulfinyl]propanoic acid)
entities_mentioned.notes: Principal non-volatile precursor compound in intact garlic cells; substrate for alliinase enzyme. Pharmacopoeial standardisation marker for dried garlic powder preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Allicin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Diallyl thiosulfinate; CAS 539-86-6
entities_mentioned.notes: Formed enzymatically from alliin by alliinase (EC 4.4.1.4) upon tissue disruption; unstable, not present in intact bulb. Primary compound responsible for characteristic odour and a major contributor to antimicrobial and cardiovascular activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Ajoene
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: (E)- and (Z)-4,5,9-trithiadodeca-1,6,11-triene 9-oxide isomers
entities_mentioned.notes: Forms predominantly in oil macerates or via acid conditions from allicin decomposition; not present in fresh or dried powder preparations. Clinically investigated for antifungal (topical) and antiplatelet uses.

entities_mentioned.name: Alliinase
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cysteine sulfoxide lyase (EC 4.4.1.4); a pyridoxal phosphate-dependent enzyme
entities_mentioned.notes: Compartmentalised in vacuoles separate from alliin in intact cells; activated upon crushing/chopping. Inactivated by heat; hence cooked garlic has substantially reduced allicin content.

entities_mentioned.name: Diallyl disulfide (DADS) / Diallyl trisulfide (DATS)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Volatile organosulfur compounds; CAS 2179-57-9 (DADS), CAS 2050-87-5 (DATS)
entities_mentioned.notes: Present in steam-distilled garlic oil; shown to have pro-apoptotic and antimicrobial activity in vitro.

entities_mentioned.name: HMG-CoA reductase
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl coenzyme A reductase (EC 1.1.1.34); rate-limiting enzyme in the mevalonate/cholesterol biosynthesis pathway
entities_mentioned.notes: Partial inhibition by garlic organosulfur compounds proposed as one mechanism underlying lipid-lowering effects; distinct from (but complementary to) statin drug mechanism.

entities_mentioned.name: Tinea pedis / Tinea corporis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Superficial dermatophyte fungal infections of the foot ('athlete's foot') and body ('ringworm'), commonly caused by Trichophyton rubrum and related species
entities_mentioned.notes: Small clinical trials with topical ajoene gel (0.4–1%) showed efficacy comparable to terbinafine 1% cream in tinea pedis in some studies; evidence still limited.

entities_mentioned.name: Warfarin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Coumarin-class oral anticoagulant (vitamin K antagonist); CAS 81-81-2
entities_mentioned.notes: Clinically significant drug-herb interaction: garlic's platelet-inhibitory effects potentiate anticoagulant action, increasing bleeding risk. Patients should inform prescribers of medicinal garlic use.

results.original_text: Radix Astragali — dried root of Astragalus membranaceus (Fisch.) Bge. or Astragalus membranaceus var. mongholicus (Bge.) Hsiao (Fabaceae). Key chemical constituents: polysaccharides (astragalans I–III, APS); triterpenoid saponins (astragalosides I–VIII, cycloastragenol); isoflavonoids (formononetin, calycosin, ononin); amino acids (γ-aminobutyric acid, canavanine); trace minerals (selenium, zinc, iron). Polysaccharide fraction typically comprises 1.5–3.0% of dried root weight. Astragaloside IV (cycloastragenol glycoside) present in trace quantities (<0.05% dry weight) but considered primary bioactive saponin.
results.translation: Astragalus root (Huang Qi) is a foundational herb in Traditional Chinese Medicine [TCM] principally employed as an immunomodulator, adaptogen, and tonic for deficiency conditions [Qi xu]. Its immunostimulant activity is primarily attributed to high-molecular-weight polysaccharides (astragalans/APS), which enhance macrophage phagocytosis, promote natural killer (NK) cell activity, stimulate T-lymphocyte proliferation, and upregulate interleukin-2 (IL-2) and interferon (IFN) production in vitro and in vivo. The triterpenoid saponin astragaloside IV has demonstrated cardioprotective, anti-fibrotic, and antioxidant effects in animal models, and has attracted modern clinical interest as a telomerase activator [TA-65 derivative]. Clinically, Radix Astragali is used as adjunct therapy in cancer patients receiving chemotherapy or radiotherapy to ameliorate immunosuppression, fatigue, and myelosuppression; in chronic renal insufficiency to reduce proteinuria and slow progression; and in recurrent upper respiratory infections as a prophylactic immune tonic. Traditional pharmacopoeial indications include chronic fatigue, spontaneous perspiration [wei qi deficiency], prolapsed organs, chronic wounds with poor healing, and edema associated with spleen-lung Qi deficiency. Pharmacological studies further report hepatoprotective effects via reduction of hepatic fibrosis markers (TGF-β1, collagen I), antihyperglycemic activity through GLUT-4 upregulation and insulin sensitization, and mild antihypertensive effects via ACE inhibition and endothelial nitric oxide modulation.

entities_mentioned.name: Astragalus membranaceus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Astragalus membranaceus (Fisch.) Bge., family Fabaceae — Mongolian milk vetch; also var. mongholicus
entities_mentioned.notes: Two botanical varieties are officially recognized in the WHO monograph and Chinese Pharmacopoeia (2020 ed.): A. membranaceus and A. membranaceus var. mongholicus. Both are used interchangeably in commerce. Native to northeastern China, Inner Mongolia, and Siberia; widely cultivated in Shanxi, Gansu, and Heilongjiang provinces.

entities_mentioned.name: Astragaloside IV
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cycloastragenol 3-O-β-D-xylopyranosyl-(1→2)-β-D-glucopyranoside; CAS 84687-43-4
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary bioactive triterpenoid saponin. Has been commercialized as TA-65 for purported telomerase activation and anti-aging applications, though clinical evidence for the latter remains preliminary and contested.

entities_mentioned.name: Astragalans (APS polysaccharides)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Heteropolysaccharide fraction including astragalan I (β-1,4-glucan backbone), II, and III; immunologically active
entities_mentioned.notes: APS fraction is the most extensively studied constituent for immunomodulatory activity. Molecular weight ranges from 12–2500 kDa depending on extraction method. In vitro studies consistently demonstrate macrophage activation and cytokine induction.

entities_mentioned.name: Formononetin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 7-hydroxy-4'-methoxyisoflavone; CAS 485-72-3
entities_mentioned.notes: Phytoestrogenic isoflavonoid. Relevant to potential interactions with hormone-sensitive conditions; contributes to hepatoprotective and antifibrotic activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Calycosin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 3'-hydroxy-4'-methoxyisoflavone; CAS 20575-57-9
entities_mentioned.notes: Isoflavonoid with reported phytoestrogenic, neuroprotective, and anti-inflammatory properties. Along with formononetin, used as a qualitative marker in pharmacopoeial standardization.

entities_mentioned.name: Huang Qi
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Traditional Chinese Medicine name for Radix Astragali (Astragalus membranaceus root)
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most frequently used tonic herbs in TCM. Classified as sweet, slightly warm; enters Lung and Spleen meridians. Listed in the Chinese Pharmacopoeia and WHO Monographs Vol. 1.

results.preparation: ORAL (dried root decoction): 9–30 g dried root per day in divided doses, decocted in water for 20–30 minutes; traditional range extends to 60 g/day in acute deficiency states under clinical supervision. ORAL (standardized extract): 250–500 mg extract (standardized to ≥0.4% astragaloside IV or ≥40% polysaccharides) two to three times daily. ORAL (fluid extract, 1:1): 4–8 mL daily. ORAL (tincture, 1:5 in 25% ethanol): 10–20 mL daily in divided doses. INJECTABLE PREPARATIONS (astragalus polysaccharide injection, Huangqi Zhusheye): used in hospital settings in China as adjunct to chemotherapy; 250 mL IV infusion — not recommended outside supervised clinical use. NOTE: WHO monograph does not specify a fixed adult dose; dosage ranges cited reflect aggregated pharmacopoeial and clinical trial data. Duration of use for immune prophylaxis: 8–12 weeks, followed by a rest period.
results.condition: Primary conditions with clinical evidence: (1) Adjunct immunosupport during cancer chemotherapy/radiotherapy — amelioration of leukopenia, fatigue, and infection risk; (2) Chronic renal insufficiency — reduction of proteinuria and renal fibrosis markers; (3) Recurrent respiratory tract infections — prophylactic reduction in frequency and severity. Traditional/pharmacopoeial indications: Qi deficiency syndromes (chronic fatigue, spontaneous sweating, shortness of breath), spleen deficiency with edema, prolapse of internal organs, non-healing ulcers and wounds, diabetes mellitus adjunct therapy.
results.source_section: WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 1 — Radix Astragali
results.translator_notes: This entry is a Plantacopia-structured synthesis derived from the WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Vol. 1 (WHO, 1999), pp. 50–61, cross-referenced with the Chinese Pharmacopoeia (2020), the European Scientific Cooperative on Phytotherapy (ESCOP) monograph on Astragali Radix (2013), and published clinical systematic reviews (e.g., Taixiang et al., 2006, Cochrane-adjacent review on astragalus-based Chinese herbal medicine in chemotherapy). The WHO monograph itself does not include a formal clinical trials section in Vol. 1; clinical evidence summarized here reflects the broader peer-reviewed literature available through 2024. Translator flags the following for editorial review: (1) Astragaloside IV telomerase activation claims (TA-65) should be labeled 'preliminary' pending Phase III RCT data — current evidence is largely from small open-label studies (Harley et al., 2011). (2) The injectable APS preparation (Huangqi Zhusheye) is a registered Chinese pharmaceutical product and is NOT covered in the WHO monograph; its inclusion here is flagged as a supplementary note and should be clearly distinguished from WHO-recommended preparations. (3) Phytoestrogenic isoflavonoid content (formononetin, calycosin) warrants a contraindication note for hormone-receptor-positive cancers — this is not explicitly stated in the 1999 WHO monograph but is recognized in subsequent literature. (4) Drug interaction potential with immunosuppressants (cyclosporine, tacrolimus) is clinically relevant and should be noted in any full Plantacopia entry: APS may antagonize iatrogenic immunosuppression.

results.original_text: Fructus Bruceae (Java brucea). Pharmaceutical source: Brucea javanica (L.) Merr. (Simaroubaceae). Dried ripe fruits. Key chemical constituents: quassinoids (bruceine A–E, bruceoside A–B, bruceolide, yadanziolide, bruceantin [~0.1–0.3% dry weight]), alkaloids (canthin-6-one derivatives, bruceajavanin A–B), fatty acids (oleic, linoleic), and trace amounts of β-carboline alkaloids. The quassinoids — particularly bruceine A, bruceine D, and bruceantin — are considered the principal bioactive constituents.
results.translation: Java brucea (Brucea javanica) is a tropical shrub whose dried ripe fruits have been used in Chinese and Southeast Asian traditional medicine primarily against malaria, amoebic dysentery, and corrosive warts (plantar verrucae). Its principal active compounds, the quassinoids, exert antiprotozoal effects by inhibiting protein synthesis in Plasmodium and Entamoeba species, and demonstrate cytotoxic and antineoplastic activity against multiple cancer cell lines through disruption of DNA topoisomerase function and induction of apoptosis. In vitro and animal studies confirm antimalarial, amoebicidal, and antitumour properties; clinical evidence — primarily from Chinese trials — supports efficacy in amoebic dysentery and as an adjunct in some gastrointestinal cancers, though large-scale randomised controlled trial data remain limited. Topical application of crushed seed paste is a traditional remedy for verruca removal, with local irritant and cytotoxic mechanisms implicated.

entities_mentioned.name: Brucea javanica
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Java brucea, Simaroubaceae family; tropical shrub native to Southeast Asia, southern China, and northern Australia
entities_mentioned.notes: Synonym Brucea sumatrana Roxb. sometimes encountered in older literature. Not to be confused with Brucea antidysenterica (Ethiopian species used similarly).

entities_mentioned.name: Bruceantin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A quassinoid isolated from Brucea javanica; CAS 41451-75-6
entities_mentioned.notes: Investigated as an antineoplastic agent in US NCI trials (1970s–1980s); clinical development halted due to narrow therapeutic index and GI toxicity.

entities_mentioned.name: Bruceine A / Bruceine D
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Quassinoid glycosides from Brucea javanica; principal antiprotozoal and cytotoxic constituents
entities_mentioned.notes: Bruceine D has shown particular potency against chloroquine-resistant Plasmodium falciparum in vitro.

entities_mentioned.name: Canthin-6-one alkaloids
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Beta-carboline-related alkaloids; also found in Picrasma and other Simaroubaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Contribute antimalarial and antifungal activity as secondary constituents.

entities_mentioned.name: Plantar verruca (corrosive wart treatment)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Human papillomavirus-induced plantar wart; topical Brucea seed paste used as a caustic/cytotoxic application
entities_mentioned.notes: Traditional topical use supported by case series; mechanism attributed to local cytotoxicity of quassinoids rather than direct antiviral action.

results.translator_notes: 1. The source prompt requests a synthesised WHO-monograph-style entry rather than a verbatim passage, so 'original_text' captures the pharmaceutical definition and constituent profile as it would appear in the WHO monograph rather than a raw OCR transcription. 2. Clinical trial data for Brucea javanica in the WHO Vol. 1 monograph is primarily from Chinese-language literature; efficacy ratings should be treated as 'limited evidence' by current GRADE standards. 3. Bruceantin's NCI investigation is historically significant but does not constitute current approved use; it is flagged here for scholarly completeness. 4. The 'preparation' and 'condition' fields requested in the user schema are embedded within 'translation' and entity notes because the JSON schema provided does not include those keys — strict schema compliance maintained. 5. Toxicity warning: all internal Brucea javanica preparations carry a risk of GI toxicity (nausea, vomiting, mucositis) and potential hepatotoxicity at supraoptimal doses; traditional dosing is tightly bounded (typically 10–15 seeds/day in Chinese pharmacopoeia guidance, encapsulated to avoid mucosal contact).

results.original_text: Radix Bupleuri — the dried root of Bupleurum falcatum L. (Apiaceae). Principal active constituents: triterpenoid saponins known as saikosaponins (saikosaponin a, c, and d; total content approximately 2–5% by dry weight); flavonoids including rutin, narcissin, and isorhamnetin; polysaccharides (bupleurans); phytosterols (α-spinasterol, stigmasterol); volatile oils; and polyacetylenes. Saikosaponin a and d are considered the primary pharmacologically active fractions.
results.translation: Thorowax root (Radix Bupleuri) is the dried root of Bupleurum falcatum L., a perennial herb in the carrot family (Apiaceae) native to East Asia, including China, Japan, and Korea, where it has been used for over two thousand years in traditional medicine. Clinically supported uses include the treatment of fever, inflammation, and hepatic disorders, with pharmacological activity attributable principally to saikosaponins, which exert anti-inflammatory effects by suppressing prostaglandin synthesis and modulating the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis to produce corticosteroid-like activity. In vitro and in vivo studies demonstrate hepatoprotective activity — saikosaponins a and d protect liver cells from chemically induced damage, stimulate bile secretion, and inhibit hepatic fibrosis, providing a mechanistic basis for traditional use in liver disease. Immunomodulatory effects, including enhancement of natural killer cell activity and macrophage stimulation, have also been documented experimentally, supporting adjunctive use in chronic inflammatory and immunological conditions. Key clinical evidence includes trials showing significant reduction in liver enzyme levels (ALT, AST) in patients with chronic hepatitis B when treated with standardised Bupleurum extracts, and favourable outcomes in combination formulae (e.g., Xiao Chai Hu Tang / Sho-saiko-to) for hepatic fibrosis and autoimmune hepatitis, though most robust data derive from combination preparations rather than the isolated root. Adverse reactions are generally mild and include nausea, increased bowel movements, and sedation at higher doses; rare but serious cases of interstitial pneumonitis have been reported with the combination formula Sho-saiko-to, particularly in patients with interferon co-administration. Contraindications include hypersensitivity to Apiaceae plants, concurrent interferon therapy, and use in patients with autoimmune conditions requiring immune suppression. The WHO-recommended adult oral dose is 3–9 g of dried root per day as a decoction, or equivalent standardised extract preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Bupleurum falcatum
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bupleurum falcatum L. (Apiaceae) — sickle-leaved hare's ear; also closely related pharmacopoeial species B. chinense DC. used interchangeably in Chinese medicine
entities_mentioned.notes: The WHO monograph primarily covers B. falcatum, but notes that B. chinense is the dominant source in Chinese Pharmacopoeia. Molecular and phytochemical profiles are closely comparable. The drug is official in the pharmacopoeias of China, Japan, and the Republic of Korea.

entities_mentioned.name: Saikosaponins (saikosaponin a, c, d)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Oleanane-type triterpenoid saponins unique to genus Bupleurum; saikosaponin a (SSa) and saikosaponin d (SSd) are the principal bioactive fractions
entities_mentioned.notes: Saikosaponin d has shown greater cytotoxicity in vitro; ratio of SSa to SSd varies with species and growing region. These are the marker compounds used for standardisation.

entities_mentioned.name: Xiao Chai Hu Tang / Sho-saiko-to
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical East Asian herbal formula (seven-herb decoction) containing Radix Bupleuri as the principal herb; studied extensively in Japan under the name Sho-saiko-to (TJ-9)
entities_mentioned.notes: Most clinical trial data for hepatoprotection and anti-fibrotic activity derive from this combination formula rather than isolated Radix Bupleuri, which complicates attribution of efficacy to the single herb. The formula is the subject of a Japanese NDA-equivalent approval.

entities_mentioned.name: Bupleurans
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: High-molecular-weight polysaccharide fractions isolated from Bupleurum root; demonstrated immunostimulatory activity in murine models
entities_mentioned.notes: Contribution to clinical efficacy in humans remains to be fully characterised; preclinical data suggest macrophage-activating and anti-tumour-promoting properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Interstitial pneumonitis (Sho-saiko-to + interferon interaction)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Drug-herb interaction: concurrent use of Sho-saiko-to formula with interferon-α in hepatitis B/C patients associated with increased risk of interstitial pneumonitis, including fatal cases reported in Japan (1990s post-marketing surveillance)
entities_mentioned.notes: This is one of the most clinically significant safety signals in the WHO monograph. The mechanism is hypothesised to involve immunological overstimulation. This interaction is the basis for the contraindication against co-administration with interferons.

results.original_text: Herba Centellae — Centella asiatica (L.) Urban (Apiaceae). Key chemical constituents: triterpenoid saponins (asiaticoside, madecassoside, brahmoside, brahminoside; collectively 1–8% dry weight); aglycones asiatic acid and madecassic acid; polyacetylenes; flavonoids (quercetin, kaempferol, rutin); essential oil (β-caryophyllene, trans-β-farnesene, germacrene D); phytosterols (stigmasterol, sitosterol); chlorogenic acid; and isothankuniside.
results.translation: Gotu kola (the dried aerial parts of Centella asiatica) is used clinically to support wound healing, improve the integrity of connective tissue in venous insufficiency, and reduce scar formation [cicatrization]. Its primary pharmacological mechanism centres on the triterpenoid saponins — asiaticoside and madecassoside — which stimulate collagen type I synthesis by dermal fibroblasts, inhibit the inflammatory phase of wound repair, and strengthen the capillary basement membrane, thereby reducing oedema and improving microcirculation in chronic venous disease. In vitro and animal studies additionally show antioxidant activity, mild anxiolytic effects mediated via GABAergic pathways, and inhibition of hypertrophic scar fibroblast proliferation. Clinical trials in patients with chronic venous hypertension and post-surgical wound healing support statistically significant improvements in symptoms (heaviness, oedema, pain) and objective capillary filtration rates at standardised triterpene doses.

entities_mentioned.name: Centella asiatica
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Centella asiatica (L.) Urban, family Apiaceae (formerly placed in Umbelliferae / Hydrocotylaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Pantropical creeping herb; the WHO monograph covers the dried whole aerial parts harvested during or after flowering. Synonyms include Hydrocotyle asiatica L. and Trisanthus cochinchinensis Lour.

entities_mentioned.name: Asiaticoside
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Triterpenoid saponin; CAS 16830-15-2; aglycone asiatic acid glycosylated with a trisaccharide (2× L-rhamnose + 1× D-glucose)
entities_mentioned.notes: Considered the principal bioactive marker for standardisation of commercial extracts (TECA, TTFCA). Promotes fibroblast collagen synthesis and accelerates wound contraction in animal models.

entities_mentioned.name: Madecassoside
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Triterpenoid saponin; CAS 34540-22-2; aglycone madecassic acid (= 6β-hydroxy-asiatic acid)
entities_mentioned.notes: Co-occurs with asiaticoside; anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties documented in vitro. Present alongside asiaticoside in the standardised extract TECA at a roughly 1:1 ratio.

entities_mentioned.name: TECA / TTFCA
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Total Triterpenic Fraction of Centella asiatica — a standardised pharmaceutical extract containing ~40% asiaticoside, ~29–30% asiatic acid, ~29–30% madecassic acid, and ~1–2% madecassoside
entities_mentioned.notes: The extract form used in the majority of controlled clinical trials for chronic venous insufficiency; marketed under trade names Madecassol® and Centellase®.

entities_mentioned.name: Herba Centellae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pharmaceutical-grade dried aerial parts of Centella asiatica; monographed in the European Pharmacopoeia and Indian Pharmacopoeia
entities_mentioned.notes: WHO definition specifies the dried plant collected during the flowering and fruiting period; moisture content not more than 10%.

results.translator_notes: **Preparation (WHO-recommended adult dosage):** (1) Dried herb / infusion: 0.33–0.67 g three times daily (total 1–2 g/day) by oral infusion or decoction. (2) Standardised dry extract (TECA/TTFCA, ~40% total triterpenes): 60–120 mg/day orally in divided doses — the dose used in positive clinical trials for chronic venous insufficiency. (3) Tincture (1:5 in 25% ethanol): 3–6 mL three times daily. (4) Topical: semi-solid preparations containing 1% total triterpenes or crude extract applied to wounds and scars 1–2× daily. **Primary conditions treated (clinical evidence-based):** Chronic venous insufficiency (CVI) / venous hypertension; wound healing acceleration; hypertrophic scar prevention; minor burns (adjunctive topical use). **Adverse reactions:** Mild gastrointestinal upset at oral doses; contact dermatitis reported with topical preparations (sensitisation to asiaticoside); rare hepatotoxicity at very high prolonged doses (case reports only). Photosensitivity possible. **Contraindications:** Hypersensitivity to Centella asiatica or other Apiaceae members; not recommended in pregnancy (emmenagogue activity in high doses in animal studies) or during lactation (insufficient safety data); avoid concurrent use with hepatotoxic drugs. **Key clinical trials summarised:** (i) Pointel et al. (1987, double-blind RCT, n=94): TTFCA 120 mg/day vs placebo over 8 weeks — significant reduction in lower-limb heaviness, oedema, and capillary filtration rate (p<0.05). (ii) Cesarone et al. (1992, controlled trial, n=52): TECA reduced capillary leakage index vs placebo in CVI patients (p<0.01). (iii) Wound healing meta-analysis context: multiple small RCTs support faster epithelialisation and reduced keloid recurrence with topical triterpene preparations. All efficacy data pertain to the standardised triterpene extract, not crude herb preparations. Source section: WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 1 — Herba Centellae.

results.original_text: Flos Chamomillae — Matricaria recutita (syn. Chamomilla recutita, Matricaria chamomilla). Key chemical constituents: essential oil (0.3–1.5%) containing (−)-α-bisabolol, bisabolol oxides A and B, bisabolone oxide A, and (E)-β-farnesene; chamazulene (formed from matricine during steam distillation); flavonoids (apigenin, luteolin, quercetin, patuletin, and their glucosides, notably apigenin-7-glucoside); mucilaginous polysaccharides; coumarins (herniarin, umbelliferone); and phenolic acids.
results.translation: Chamomile flower (Flos Chamomillae) consists of the dried flowerheads of Matricaria recutita L. (family Asteraceae), an annual herb native to southern and eastern Europe and western Asia and widely cultivated worldwide. The plant is recognised for its anti-inflammatory, antispasmodic, carminative, and mild sedative properties. Its principal mechanisms of action include: (1) inhibition of cyclooxygenase and lipoxygenase pathways by chamazulene and α-bisabolol, reducing prostaglandin and leukotriene synthesis; (2) binding of apigenin to central benzodiazepine receptors, contributing to anxiolytic and mild sedative effects; (3) spasmolytic activity on smooth muscle mediated by flavonoids and α-bisabolol, relieving gastrointestinal cramping; and (4) promotion of wound healing through α-bisabolol-induced acceleration of epithelial regeneration. Clinically, chamomile preparations are used for symptomatic relief of minor gastrointestinal disturbances (flatulence, bloating, dyspepsia, spastic colitis), mild anxiety and insomnia, and topically for inflammatory skin and mucous-membrane conditions. Traditional pharmacopoeias also record its use in paediatric colic and teething discomfort.

entities_mentioned.name: Matricaria recutita
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: German chamomile (Asteraceae); synonyms include Chamomilla recutita (L.) Rauschert and Matricaria chamomilla L.
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinct from Roman (English) chamomile, Chamaemelum nobile (L.) All., which is a separate species with a different chemical profile. WHO monographs treat these as separate entries.

entities_mentioned.name: chamazulene
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sesquiterpene hydrocarbon (CAS 529-05-5) formed by steam distillation from the pro-compound matricine; responsible for the characteristic deep blue colour of distilled chamomile oil.
entities_mentioned.notes: Not present as such in the fresh plant; an artefact of distillation, yet pharmacologically active as a COX/LOX inhibitor.

entities_mentioned.name: α-bisabolol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: (−)-α-Bisabolol (levomenol); monocyclic sesquiterpene alcohol considered a primary marker of quality in chamomile essential oil.
entities_mentioned.notes: Commercially available as a pure isolate (levomenol) used in dermatological formulations; anti-inflammatory and wound-healing agent in vitro and in vivo.

entities_mentioned.name: apigenin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 4′,5,7-trihydroxyflavone; predominant flavonoid aglycone in chamomile; ligand at central benzodiazepine-binding site of GABA-A receptors.
entities_mentioned.notes: Apigenin-7-glucoside (apigetrin) is the principal glycoside form in the intact plant; hydrolysis in the gut releases the aglycone for systemic absorption.

entities_mentioned.name: bisabolol oxides A and B
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Oxygenated sesquiterpene derivatives of α-bisabolol found in chamomile essential oil; considered adulterant markers when present in high proportion relative to α-bisabolol.
entities_mentioned.notes: High bisabolol oxide content relative to α-bisabolol may indicate use of Ormenis multicaulis (Moroccan chamomile) as an adulterant.

entities_mentioned.name: herniarin and umbelliferone
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Furanocoumarins / simple coumarins present in chamomile; umbelliferone (7-hydroxycoumarin) and herniarin (7-methoxycoumarin).
entities_mentioned.notes: Photosensitisation potential at high topical doses is theoretically noted but considered negligible at normal therapeutic concentrations.

results.translator_notes: 1. PREPARATION & DOSAGE (WHO-recommended, adults): (a) Infusion (internal use): 3 g dried flowerheads per 150 ml boiling water, steeped 5–10 min, taken 3–4 times daily between meals; (b) Liquid extract (1:1 in 45% ethanol): 1–4 ml three times daily; (c) Tincture (1:5 in 45% ethanol): 3–10 ml three times daily; (d) Essential oil (internal): 0.3–0.6 ml daily in divided doses; (e) Semi-solid topical preparations: 3–10% chamomile extract (w/w) applied 1–3 times daily to affected skin or mucous membranes; (f) Mouth rinse / gargle: 1–2% infusion or diluted liquid extract. CONDITIONS (clinical-evidence tier): Gastrointestinal spasm and dyspepsia; anxiety and mild insomnia (apigenin-mediated); inflammatory dermatoses, wound healing, and oral mucositis (topical). CONDITIONS (traditional / pharmacopoeial tier): Infantile colic, teething discomfort, conjunctivitis (eyewash), haemorrhoids (topical), dysmenorrhoea. ADVERSE REACTIONS: Allergic contact dermatitis and, rarely, anaphylaxis in individuals sensitised to other Asteraceae (cross-reactivity with ragweed, chrysanthemum, feverfew). Reported allergens include anthecotulide and other sesquiterpene lactones. CONTRAINDICATIONS: Known hypersensitivity to chamomile or other members of the family Asteraceae/Compositae. Internal use in infants and toddlers should follow paediatric medical guidance. DRUG INTERACTIONS: Theoretical potentiation of warfarin anticoagulation (coumarin constituents); additive CNS depression with benzodiazepines or other sedatives (apigenin); use with caution in combination. 2. results.original_text: Cortex Cinnamomi — Cinnamomum verum J. Presl (syn. C. zeylanicum Blume), family Lauraceae. Principal chemical constituents: volatile oil (1–4%, predominantly trans-cinnamaldehyde 55–90%), cinnamate esters, proanthocyanidins (type-A polymers), diterpenes (cinnzeylanol, cinnzeylanine), coumarin (trace, higher in C. cassia), eugenol (bark oil up to 10%), and polyphenolic polymers with insulin-potentiating activity.
results.translation: Cinnamon bark [Cortex Cinnamomi] is derived from the dried inner bark of Cinnamomum verum and has been used medicinally since antiquity across South Asian, Chinese, and European traditions. Its primary pharmacologically active constituents are trans-cinnamaldehyde, which exerts antimicrobial and antifungal effects by disrupting microbial membrane integrity, and type-A proanthocyanidin polymers, which have been shown in vitro and in small clinical trials to potentiate insulin signalling by up-regulating insulin-receptor kinase activity and inhibiting glycogen synthase kinase-3β. Cinnamaldehyde also produces antispasmodic effects on smooth muscle, supporting traditional use for gastrointestinal complaints such as dyspepsia, flatulence, and mild spasmodic colitis. Anti-inflammatory activity has been documented via inhibition of arachidonic acid metabolism and prostaglandin biosynthesis, which may contribute to analgesic effects observed in traditional use.

entities_mentioned.name: Cinnamomum verum
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: True cinnamon / Ceylon cinnamon (Lauraceae); distinct from Chinese cinnamon, Cinnamomum cassia, which contains higher coumarin levels and differs in safety profile
entities_mentioned.notes: WHO Monographs Vol. 1 treats Cortex Cinnamomi as specifically derived from C. verum; monographs elsewhere address C. cassia separately. Coumarin content in C. verum is negligible, reducing hepatotoxicity risk compared to C. cassia.

entities_mentioned.name: trans-cinnamaldehyde
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Primary phenylpropanoid aldehyde constituent of cinnamon bark volatile oil; CAS 14371-10-9
entities_mentioned.notes: Responsible for characteristic aroma; principal antimicrobial and antifungal active; concentration 55–90% of essential oil fraction.

entities_mentioned.name: Type-A proanthocyanidins
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Polyphenolic oligomers/polymers with doubly-linked interflavanol bonds; documented as insulin-potentiating fraction in cinnamon
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinguished from more common type-B proanthocyanidins by an additional ether linkage. Key fraction in glycaemic activity research (Anderson et al., 2004).

entities_mentioned.name: Eugenol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 4-allyl-2-methoxyphenol; a phenylpropanoid present in bark oil up to ~10%; also dominant in clove oil
entities_mentioned.notes: Contributes to antispasmodic and local anaesthetic properties; potential allergen at high doses — relevant to adverse reaction profile.

entities_mentioned.name: Coumarin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 1,2-benzopyrone; hepatotoxic at high intake; present in trace amounts in C. verum but significantly higher in C. cassia
entities_mentioned.notes: European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) flagged coumarin in C. cassia as a hepatotoxicity risk; C. verum is considered safe at normal dietary and medicinal doses.

Clinicians should note that adulteration with or substitution by C. cassia is common commercially and alters the coumarin/safety profile substantially. Contraindicated in pregnancy (uterine stimulant effects at medicinal doses) and in patients with known allergy to Cinnamomum spp. or balsam of Peru (cross-reactivity via cinnamate sensitisation).

results.original_text: Rhizoma Coptidis — Coptis chinensis Franch. (Ranunculaceae). Major chemical constituents: berberine (5–8% dry weight), coptisine, palmatine, jatrorrhizine, epiberberine, columbamine, magnoflorine, worenine. Isoquinoline alkaloids predominate, with berberine as the primary pharmacologically active compound.
results.translation: Goldthread rhizome (the dried root and rhizome of Coptis chinensis) is used clinically and traditionally to treat gastrointestinal infections, bacterial diarrhoea, and inflammatory conditions of the digestive tract. Its principal active compound, berberine [an isoquinoline alkaloid], exerts broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity by inhibiting bacterial DNA gyrase and disrupting cell membrane integrity, while also demonstrating anti-inflammatory effects through inhibition of NF-κB signalling and suppression of pro-inflammatory cytokines. Berberine additionally activates AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK), which underlies observed antidiabetic and lipid-lowering effects documented in clinical trials. Traditional pharmacopoeias (Chinese, Japanese, Korean) further record use for fever, conjunctivitis ["red eye"], and oral ulceration, consistent with the plant's antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory profile.

entities_mentioned.name: Rhizoma Coptidis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried rhizome and root of Coptis chinensis Franch. (family Ranunculaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Pharmaceutical Latin name used in WHO monographs, Chinese Pharmacopoeia, and Japanese Pharmacopoeia. Three species are officially recognised as sources: C. chinensis, C. deltoidea, and C. teeta.

entities_mentioned.name: Coptis chinensis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Coptis chinensis Franch., Ranunculaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Small perennial herb native to the mountain forests of central and southwestern China (Sichuan, Yunnan, Guizhou provinces); rhizomes are the official medicinal part.

entities_mentioned.name: Berberine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Berberine hydrochloride (C20H18NO4+); isoquinoline alkaloid, CAS 2086-83-1
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary active constituent, present at 5–8% in dried rhizome by HPLC. Clinically studied for type 2 diabetes, dyslipidaemia, and infectious diarrhoea. Listed in multiple national pharmacopoeias as a reference marker compound for quality control of Rhizoma Coptidis.

entities_mentioned.name: Coptisine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Coptisine chloride; isoquinoline alkaloid structurally related to berberine
entities_mentioned.notes: Second major alkaloid; contributes to antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Palmatine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Palmatine; protoberberine-type isoquinoline alkaloid
entities_mentioned.notes: Demonstrated antimicrobial and antioxidant activity in vitro.

entities_mentioned.name: Jatrorrhizine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Jatrorrhizine; protoberberine alkaloid
entities_mentioned.notes: Minor constituent with reported antifungal properties.

entities_mentioned.name: NF-κB
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nuclear factor kappa-light-chain-enhancer of activated B cells; transcription factor central to inflammatory signalling
entities_mentioned.notes: Berberine inhibits NF-κB activation, reducing transcription of TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6.

entities_mentioned.name: AMPK
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: AMP-activated protein kinase; master regulator of cellular energy homeostasis
entities_mentioned.notes: Berberine activates AMPK independently of insulin signalling, accounting for glucose-lowering and lipid-lowering effects observed in type 2 diabetes clinical trials.

results.preparation: DEFINITION: Dried rhizome and fibrous roots of Coptis chinensis Franch. (or C. deltoidea C.Y. Cheng et Hsiao, or C. teeta Wall.), family Ranunculaceae, collected in autumn, cleaned, and dried. BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION: Perennial herb 15–35 cm tall; leaves basal, trifoliate, with deeply incised leaflets; flowers small, white-yellowish; rhizome beaded, bright yellow internally due to berberine content; fibrous roots numerous, forming a "chicken-foot" cluster (Chinese: 鸡爪黄连). GEOGRAPHICAL DISTRIBUTION: Native to mountainous regions of central China (Sichuan, Hubei, Guizhou); cultivated extensively in Sichuan province; related species found in Japan and Himalayan regions. DOSAGE FORMS AND RECOMMENDED DOSES (WHO adult dosage): (1) Dried crude drug (decoction): 1.5–9 g per day in divided doses, prepared as aqueous decoction. (2) Powdered drug (oral): 1–3 g per day. (3) Standardised extract (oral): dose equivalent to 0.9–1.5 g crude drug, taken 2–3 times daily. (4) Berberine hydrochloride tablet (isolated alkaloid): 0.3–0.9 g per day in divided doses for infectious diarrhoea. ADVERSE REACTIONS: Nausea, vomiting, abdominal discomfort at high doses; rare hypersensitivity reactions; neonatal haemolytic anaemia and jaundice (bilirubin displacement by berberine — contraindicated in neonates). DRUG INTERACTIONS: May potentiate anticoagulant effects of warfarin; may inhibit CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein, increasing plasma levels of co-administered drugs metabolised by these pathways (e.g., cyclosporin, statins). CONTRAINDICATIONS: Pregnancy (uterotonic effects reported in animal studies); neonates and infants (risk of haemolytic jaundice); known hypersensitivity to Coptis species or berberine. Not recommended in cases of deficiency-cold syndromes per traditional Chinese medicine diagnosis.
results.condition: Primary conditions (clinical evidence-based): (1) Infectious and non-specific diarrhoea [Clostridium, E. coli, Vibrio cholerae — antimicrobial mechanism]; (2) Type 2 diabetes mellitus [glycaemic control; RCT evidence showing HbA1c reduction comparable to metformin 500 mg TID in several trials]; (3) Dyslipidaemia [LDL-C and triglyceride reduction via PCSK9 inhibition and AMPK activation]; (4) Gastrointestinal inflammation and ulceration [anti-inflammatory, mucosal protective effects]. Traditional uses (pharmacopoeial): fever and heat toxicity syndromes, conjunctivitis, oral ulcers, hypertension.

Three clinical trial highlights are embedded in the translation and condition fields: (A) Zhang et al. (2008, J Clin Endocrinol Metab): berberine 500 mg TID reduced HbA1c by 2.0% vs. metformin 2.0% over 3 months in 116 T2DM patients; (B) Kong et al. (2004, J Cardiovasc Pharmacol): berberine 0.5 g BID significantly reduced LDL-C and triglycerides vs. placebo; (C) Hua et al. (1999, Chin Med J): berberine tablets non-inferior to tetracycline for acute bacterial diarrhoea in RCT of 165 patients. The source prompt did not supply verbatim WHO monograph text; accordingly, all pharmacological detail is drawn from the published WHO Monographs Vol. 1 structure and peer-reviewed literature consistent with that monograph. Genuinely illegible passages: none (no OCR source text was provided). results.original_text: Rhizoma Curcumae Longae (Curcuma longa L., Zingiberaceae). Primary chemical constituents: curcuminoids (curcumin, demethoxycurcumin, bisdemethoxycurcumin; collectively 2–9% dry weight of rhizome); volatile oils (4–14%), principally turmerone, atlantone, and zingiberene; polysaccharides (ukonan A–D); fixed oils; resins.
results.translation: Turmeric (Curcuma longa) is a perennial herb of the ginger family (Zingiberaceae), native to South and Southeast Asia and widely cultivated throughout tropical regions. The medicinal part used is the dried, processed rhizome (underground stem), which is official in numerous national pharmacopoeias. Its principal active constituents are the curcuminoids — chiefly curcumin — which give the rhizome its characteristic deep yellow colour, together with an essential oil fraction rich in sesquiterpene ketones (turmerones). Pharmacologically, curcumin has been demonstrated to inhibit the arachidonic acid cascade (reducing prostaglandin and leukotriene synthesis), to scavenge reactive oxygen species [antioxidant activity], to suppress nuclear factor kappa-B (NF-κB) signalling, and to inhibit platelet aggregation in vitro and in vivo. These mechanisms collectively account for documented anti-inflammatory, hepatoprotective, choleretic [bile-stimulating], carminative [gas-relieving], and antimicrobial effects. Clinical trials support its use as an anti-inflammatory agent comparable in some measures to phenylbutazone and cortisone (without the associated adverse profile), and as a choleretic agent in biliary [gallbladder/bile-duct] dysfunction. Traditional and pharmacopoeial uses additionally include dyspepsia, flatulence, liver complaints, and topical wound healing. Adverse reactions are minimal at recommended doses; prolonged high-dose use may cause gastric irritation. Turmeric is contraindicated in cases of biliary obstruction and should be used with caution in patients with gallstones. No significant drug interactions are established at therapeutic doses, though theoretical interactions with anticoagulants exist given its antiplatelet activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Rhizoma Curcumae Longae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried rhizome of Curcuma longa L. (Zingiberaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Pharmaceutical Latin name used in WHO Monographs and multiple national pharmacopoeias (Indian, British, European, Japanese). The rhizome is the official part; leaves and aerial parts are not included in this monograph definition.

entities_mentioned.name: Curcuma longa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Turmeric; family Zingiberaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Synonyms include Curcuma domestica Valeton. Widely naturalised beyond its South Asian origin; major commercial production in India, China, Indonesia, and Jamaica.

entities_mentioned.name: Curcumin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Diferuloylmethane; CAS 458-37-7; primary curcuminoid of C. longa
entities_mentioned.notes: Constitutes approximately 60–70% of the total curcuminoid fraction. Poor oral bioavailability in isolation; piperine (from black pepper) is known to enhance absorption substantially. Key subject of anti-inflammatory and antioxidant pharmacological research.

entities_mentioned.name: Turmerone
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ar-turmerone and α/β-turmerone; major sesquiterpene ketones of turmeric essential oil
entities_mentioned.notes: Principal components of the volatile oil fraction alongside atlantone and zingiberene. Contribute to the characteristic odour and possess independent antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Ukonan A–D
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acidic polysaccharides isolated from Curcuma longa rhizome
entities_mentioned.notes: Reported to exhibit immunostimulating and anti-tumour activity in preclinical (murine) models; not yet the subject of robust clinical trials.

entities_mentioned.name: NF-κB
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nuclear factor kappa-light-chain-enhancer of activated B cells; transcription factor central to inflammatory signalling
entities_mentioned.notes: Curcumin's inhibition of NF-κB is one of the most extensively replicated mechanisms in the curcumin pharmacology literature and is proposed to underlie multiple downstream anti-inflammatory and potential anti-tumour effects.

No OCR artefacts were present to correct. 2. DOSAGE (not captured in preparation field due to schema): WHO-recommended adult oral doses are: crude drug (dried rhizome powder) 1.5–3 g/day; standardised extract (curcumin) typically 400–600 mg three times daily in clinical trial contexts; tincture (1:10, 70% ethanol) 10 mL three times daily. 3. Robust large-scale RCTs remain limited as of WHO Volume 1 publication date. 5. results.original_text: Radix Echinaceae Pallidae — dried root and rhizome of Echinacea pallida (Nutt.) Nutt. (Asteraceae). Key chemical constituents: caffeic acid derivatives (echinacoside, 0.5–1.0% dry weight; 1,3-O-dicaffeoylquinic acid; chlorogenic acid), polyacetylenes (ketoalkenes and ketoalkynes, notably pentadeca-(8Z)-en-2-one), polysaccharides (heteroglycans, arabinogalactans), alkylamides (present in low or trace concentrations, distinguishing E. pallida from E. purpurea and E. angustifolia), and essential oils.
results.translation: Pale coneflower root (Echinacea pallida) is used primarily to support the immune system during upper respiratory tract infections, including the common cold and influenza-like illness, by reducing both the severity and duration of symptoms. Its principal pharmacological mechanism is immunomodulation: the caffeic acid derivatives — especially echinacoside — and high-molecular-weight polysaccharides stimulate macrophage activation, enhance phagocytosis, and promote cytokine release (including interferon and interleukin production) in vitro and in vivo. Unlike E. purpurea and E. angustifolia, E. pallida root contains little to no alkylamides, so its immunostimulant activity is attributed chiefly to its echinacoside and polysaccharide fractions. Clinical evidence, including randomised controlled trials, supports a statistically significant reduction in the duration of cold symptoms (mean reduction approximately 1–4 days) when a hydroalcoholic [ethanolic] tincture is administered at onset of illness. Traditional pharmacopoeial use also extends to adjunctive support for chronic infections and superficial wound healing, though evidence for these applications is weaker. Adverse reactions are uncommon at recommended doses; hypersensitivity reactions (including urticaria and, rarely, anaphylaxis) may occur, particularly in individuals with atopic conditions or known allergy to other Asteraceae family members. The plant is contraindicated in progressive systemic diseases such as tuberculosis, leukaemia, collagenosis, multiple sclerosis, and autoimmune disorders, as well as in patients receiving immunosuppressant therapy. Use in pregnancy and lactation is not recommended due to insufficient safety data.

entities_mentioned.name: Radix Echinaceae Pallidae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried root and rhizome of Echinacea pallida (Nutt.) Nutt., family Asteraceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Pharmaceutical-grade material is defined by the presence of echinacoside as a marker compound; WHO monograph specifies minimum 0.5% echinacoside by HPLC in the dried root. Distinct from E. purpurea root (Radix Echinaceae Purpureae) and E. angustifolia root by chemotype: low alkylamide content.

entities_mentioned.name: Echinacea pallida
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Echinacea pallida (Nutt.) Nutt. — Pale purple coneflower, native to central North America (Great Plains, prairies of the US Midwest)
entities_mentioned.notes: Perennial herb, 60–100 cm tall; ray florets pale pink to white, drooping; native from Nebraska and Kansas east to Ohio; widely cultivated in Europe for medicinal root production. Morphologically distinguished from E. purpurea by narrower, 3–5-veined leaves and drooping ray petals.

entities_mentioned.name: Echinacoside
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Caffeic acid glycoside; 3-O-(β-D-glucopyranosyl-(1→6)-4-O-caffeoyl-β-D-glucopyranosyl)caffeic acid ester of 3,4-dihydroxyphenylethanol
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary marker compound for E. pallida root quality control; reported at 0.5–1.0% in dried root. Demonstrates antioxidant, antiviral, and indirect immunostimulant activity in vitro.

entities_mentioned.name: Polyacetylenes (ketoalkenes/ketoalkynes)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pentadeca-(8Z)-en-2-one and related C15 polyacetylenic compounds
entities_mentioned.notes: Considered secondary markers for E. pallida root authenticity; may contribute antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory activity. Distinguish E. pallida from E. angustifolia which contains different polyacetylene profiles.

entities_mentioned.name: Arabinogalactans / Heteroglycans
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: High-molecular-weight polysaccharides isolated from Echinacea spp. root
entities_mentioned.notes: Demonstrated macrophage activation and complement fixation activity in vitro; considered key contributors to immunomodulatory effect alongside caffeic acid derivatives.

entities_mentioned.name: Asteraceae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Compositae — daisy family
entities_mentioned.notes: Taxonomic family relevant to contraindication: patients with known allergy to Asteraceae members (e.g., ragweed [Ambrosia], chrysanthemum, daisy) are at elevated risk of hypersensitivity reaction to Echinacea preparations.

results.original_text: Herba Echinaceae Purpureae — aerial parts of Echinacea purpurea (L.) Moench (Asteraceae), harvested during flowering. Key chemical constituents: caffeic acid derivatives (cichoric acid, up to 2.1%; caftaric acid; chlorogenic acid), alkylamides (isobutylamides of polyunsaturated fatty acids), polysaccharides (heteroxylan, arabinogalactan), glycoproteins, essential oil (humulene, germacrene D), and flavonoids (rutin, quercetin). Echinacoside absent in E. purpurea (characteristic of E. pallida/angustifolia).
results.translation: Purple coneflower (Echinacea purpurea) is a North American prairie herb whose above-ground parts [herba] are used medicinally primarily to support immune function. Clinically, it is best supported as a short-term treatment for reducing the severity and duration of the common cold and upper respiratory tract infections [URTIs], with several randomized controlled trials demonstrating earlier symptom resolution compared to placebo. Pharmacologically, its active constituents — particularly alkylamides and caffeic acid derivatives such as cichoric acid — stimulate macrophage activation, enhance phagocytosis, increase natural killer (NK) cell activity, and promote cytokine production (especially interferon-beta and interleukin-1), thereby amplifying innate immune responses. Polysaccharides and glycoproteins contribute additional immunomodulatory and mild anti-inflammatory effects in vitro and in animal models. Traditional uses extend to wound healing, urinary tract infections, and adjunctive support during bacterial or viral illness, though clinical evidence is strongest for URTI prevention and symptomatic relief. It is contraindicated in individuals with progressive systemic diseases (e.g., tuberculosis, multiple sclerosis, HIV/AIDS, collagen disorders, autoimmune conditions) and in those with known hypersensitivity to Asteraceae plants. It is not recommended for use beyond eight consecutive weeks, as prolonged immunostimulation has not been adequately studied and theoretical immunosuppressive rebound is a concern.

entities_mentioned.name: Echinacea purpurea
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Echinacea purpurea (L.) Moench, family Asteraceae; eastern North American native, widely cultivated in Europe and North America
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinct from E. angustifolia and E. pallida; E. purpurea lacks echinacoside, which is a key chemical differentiator. The aerial parts (Herba) rather than root are the official plant part per this WHO monograph.

entities_mentioned.name: Cichoric acid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dicaffeoyltartaric acid; primary caffeic acid derivative marker compound for E. purpurea herba
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a standardization marker in European pharmacopoeial preparations; associated with antiviral and immunostimulant activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Alkylamides
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Isobutylamides of C11–C16 polyunsaturated fatty acids; e.g., undeca-2E,4Z-diene-8,10-diynoic acid isobutylamide
entities_mentioned.notes: Considered among the most bioactive lipophilic constituents; responsible for the characteristic tingling sensation on the tongue. Show affinity for cannabinoid receptor CB2, contributing to immunomodulation.

entities_mentioned.name: Arabinogalactan
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: High-molecular-weight polysaccharide fraction isolated from E. purpurea cell cultures and aerial parts
entities_mentioned.notes: Demonstrated macrophage activation and interferon induction in vitro.

entities_mentioned.name: Echinacoside
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Caffeic acid glycoside; marker compound for E. angustifolia and E. pallida roots — absent in E. purpurea
entities_mentioned.notes: Frequently cited in literature but must not be used as a quality marker for E. purpurea preparations; its presence in a purported E. purpurea product may indicate adulteration.

entities_mentioned.name: Upper respiratory tract infection (URTI)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Common cold and related viral or bacterial upper airway illness
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary evidence-based indication per WHO monograph; multiple RCTs show reduced duration and symptom severity.

results.original_text: Herba Ephedrae (Ephedra sinica Stapf, family Ephedraceae). Primary chemical constituents: total alkaloids 1–3% of dry weight, principally l-ephedrine (30–90% of total alkaloid fraction) and d-pseudoephedrine, with minor alkaloids including norephedrine, norpseudoephedrine, methylephedrine, and methylpseudoephedrine. Additional constituents include tannins, flavonoids (including herbacetin glycosides), volatile oils, and polysaccharides.
results.translation:
Herba Ephedrae consists of the dried young stems (herbaceous aerial parts) of Ephedra sinica Stapf and related species (E. intermedia Schrenk & C.A. Meyer; E. equisetina Bunge), harvested in autumn. The plant is a low-growing, broom-like gymnosperm shrub with scale-like leaves and jointed, green, photosynthetic stems; it is native to arid and semi-arid regions of northern China, Mongolia, and central Asia, and has been cultivated in China for over 5,000 years.

The principal pharmacological activity is attributable to l-ephedrine and d-pseudoephedrine. Ephedrine acts as a sympathomimetic agent through both direct agonism at α- and β-adrenergic receptors and indirect stimulation via norepinephrine release from presynaptic terminals; this dual mechanism produces bronchodilation [relaxation of bronchial smooth muscle via β₂-adrenoceptor activation], vasoconstriction, cardiac stimulation [positive chronotropic and inotropic effects], and central nervous system stimulation. Pseudoephedrine exerts predominantly indirect sympathomimetic effects with somewhat greater selectivity for nasal vasculature, accounting for its use as a nasal decongestant.

Clinical evidence from randomised controlled trials and systematic reviews supports the short-term efficacy of ephedrine and pseudoephedrine as bronchodilators in mild-to-moderate asthma and as nasal decongestants in upper respiratory conditions. A Cochrane-style review of ephedrine in asthma demonstrated statistically significant improvements in forced expiratory volume (FEV₁) and peak expiratory flow rate (PEFR) versus placebo, though effect sizes were modest compared to selective β₂-agonists. Studies of ephedrine-containing weight-loss preparations (commonly combined with caffeine) showed short-term reductions in body weight in obese subjects, but with an unfavourable cardiovascular adverse-event profile that substantially limits clinical utility. Traditional pharmacopoeial use in Chinese medicine (recorded in Shennong Bencao Jing, c. 100 CE) includes treatment of asthma [wheezing, dyspnoea], common cold with chills, fever without sweating, oedema, and arthritic pain.

Known adverse reactions include hypertension, tachycardia, palpitations, cardiac arrhythmia, insomnia, nervousness, tremor, headache, urinary retention, and — at high doses or in susceptible individuals — stroke, myocardial infarction, and death. Drug interactions of clinical significance include: monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs; risk of hypertensive crisis), cardiac glycosides (increased risk of arrhythmia), halothane anaesthesia (arrhythmia), guanethidine (antagonism of hypotensive effect), and other sympathomimetic agents (additive toxicity). Contraindications include: hypertension, cardiovascular disease, hyperthyroidism, angle-closure glaucoma, pheochromocytoma, prostatic hyperplasia with urinary retention, anxiety disorders, concurrent MAOI therapy, and pregnancy. Use is contraindicated in children under 12 years. The WHO-recommended adult oral dose of the crude drug is 1.5–9 g per day as a decoction; standardised preparations should be dosed according to alkaloid content, typically providing 15–30 mg total alkaloids per dose, not to exceed three doses daily, and not for use beyond 7 consecutive days without medical supervision.

entities_mentioned.name: Ephedra sinica
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ephedra sinica Stapf (Ephedraceae); Ma huang
entities_mentioned.notes: WHO Monograph Vol. 1 also covers E. intermedia Schrenk & C.A. Meyer and E. equisetina Bunge as acceptable source species for Herba Ephedrae.

entities_mentioned.name: l-ephedrine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: (-)-ephedrine; CAS 299-42-3; sympathomimetic alkaloid
entities_mentioned.notes: Principal active alkaloid; direct and indirect α/β-adrenergic agonist. Constitutes 30–90% of total alkaloid content depending on species and harvest time.

entities_mentioned.name: d-pseudoephedrine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: (+)-pseudoephedrine; CAS 90-82-4; sympathomimetic alkaloid
entities_mentioned.notes: Second major alkaloid; predominantly indirect sympathomimetic; widely used pharmaceutically as nasal decongestant.

entities_mentioned.name: norephedrine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: phenylpropanolamine; CAS 14838-15-4
entities_mentioned.notes: Minor alkaloid in Herba Ephedrae; formerly used as appetite suppressant; withdrawn from many markets due to haemorrhagic stroke risk.

entities_mentioned.name: Shennong Bencao Jing
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classic of the Divine Farmer's Materia Medica; earliest extant Chinese pharmacopoeia, compiled c. 100 CE
entities_mentioned.notes: Earliest recorded pharmacopoeial reference to Ma huang; documents use for respiratory conditions and oedema.

entities_mentioned.name: herbacetin glycosides
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Flavonoid glycosides of herbacetin (3,4′,5,7,8-pentahydroxyflavone)
entities_mentioned.notes: Non-alkaloid secondary metabolites in Herba Ephedrae; limited pharmacological data in WHO source; antioxidant activity noted in vitro.

results.original_text: Folium Ginkgo — Ginkgo biloba L. (Ginkgoaceae). Key chemical constituents: flavonoid glycosides (22–27%, principally quercetin, kaempferol, and isorhamnetin glycosides including ginkgoflavone glycosides); terpene trilactones (5–7%, comprising ginkgolides A, B, C, J and bilobalide); biflavones (amentoflavone, bilobetin, ginkgetin, isoginkgetin, sciadopitysin); proanthocyanidins; ginkgolic acids (alkylphenols, kept to <5 ppm in standardised extracts due to potential allergenicity). Standardised dry extract (EGb 761 and LI 1370): 22–27% flavone glycosides, 5–7% terpene lactones.
results.translation: Ginkgo leaf (Folium Ginkgo) is the dried leaf of Ginkgo biloba L., a living-fossil tree native to eastern China and now cultivated globally for both ornamental and medicinal purposes. The leaf is fan-shaped with a distinctive bilobed apex and parallel venation, and it is harvested in autumn from trees that may live over a thousand years. Medicinally, standardised ginkgo leaf extracts are used primarily to treat symptoms of cognitive decline associated with dementia (including Alzheimer's type and multi-infarct dementia), cerebrovascular insufficiency, and peripheral arterial occlusive disease [intermittent claudication]. Pharmacologically, ginkgo's flavonoid fraction acts as a potent free-radical scavenger and antioxidant, protecting neuronal and vascular membranes from oxidative damage, while the terpene trilactones — most critically ginkgolide B — are selective antagonists of platelet-activating factor (PAF), inhibiting platelet aggregation and reducing pathological thrombosis. Together these fractions improve microcirculation, increase cerebral and peripheral blood flow, modulate neurotransmitter systems (including cholinergic and serotonergic pathways), and exert neuroprotective effects demonstrated in both in vitro cell models and in vivo animal studies. Multiple randomised controlled trials (including the landmark 1997 Le Bars et al. JAMA trial, n=309) have documented statistically significant stabilisation or modest improvement in cognitive function and activities of daily living in patients with mild-to-moderate dementia over 26–52 weeks of treatment with standardised EGb 761 extract at 120–240 mg/day, compared to placebo. Adverse reactions are generally mild and include gastrointestinal upset, headache, and allergic skin reactions; rare but serious events include bleeding complications, particularly when used concurrently with anticoagulants (warfarin) or antiplatelet agents (aspirin, clopidogrel). Ginkgolic acids, present in crude leaf preparations, are contact allergens and potential mutagens, and are reduced to below 5 ppm in pharmaceutical-grade extracts. Ginkgo leaf preparations are contraindicated in known hypersensitivity to Ginkgo biloba preparations, and should be used with caution in patients with coagulation disorders or those taking anticoagulant or antiplatelet therapy. The WHO-recommended adult dosage of standardised dry extract (24% flavone glycosides, 6% terpene lactones) is 120–240 mg dry extract per day in two to three divided oral doses; crude leaf preparations and teas are not considered clinically equivalent to standardised extracts and are not recommended for therapeutic use in dementia or vascular indications.

entities_mentioned.name: Ginkgo biloba
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ginkgo biloba L. (family Ginkgoaceae) — the sole living species of the order Ginkgoales; a gymnosperm sometimes called 'maidenhair tree'
entities_mentioned.notes: Regarded as a 'living fossil'; cultivated widely in China, France, South Carolina (USA), and Japan for pharmaceutical leaf production. EGb 761 (Dr. Willmar Schwabe GmbH) and LI 1370 (Lichtwer Pharma) are the two most widely studied standardised extracts.

entities_mentioned.name: Ginkgolide B
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A diterpene trilactone unique to Ginkgo biloba; a highly selective platelet-activating factor (PAF) receptor antagonist
entities_mentioned.notes: Considered the primary antiplatelet and neuroprotective terpenoid constituent; structurally complex with five rings including a spiro[4.4]nonane core.

entities_mentioned.name: Bilobalide
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A sesquiterpene trilactone constituent of Ginkgo biloba leaf
entities_mentioned.notes: Demonstrated neuroprotective effects in ischaemia models in vitro and in vivo; may protect mitochondrial function.

entities_mentioned.name: EGb 761
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Standardised dry extract of Ginkgo biloba leaf (Dr. Willmar Schwabe GmbH & Co.); 22–27% flavone glycosides, 5–7% terpene lactones, <5 ppm ginkgolic acids
entities_mentioned.notes: The most extensively studied proprietary extract; used in the 1997 Le Bars et al. JAMA multicentre RCT for Alzheimer's dementia.

entities_mentioned.name: Ginkgolic acids
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Long-chain alkylphenols (anacardic acid analogues) present in Ginkgo biloba leaf and seed
entities_mentioned.notes: Known contact allergens and cytotoxic/potentially genotoxic compounds; pharmaceutical-grade extracts restrict these to <5 ppm per European Pharmacopoeia and WHO guidance.

entities_mentioned.name: Platelet-activating factor (PAF)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 1-O-alkyl-2-acetyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine; a potent phospholipid mediator of inflammation, platelet aggregation, and bronchoconstriction
entities_mentioned.notes: Ginkgolides are among the most potent naturally occurring PAF antagonists identified; PAF antagonism underlies much of ginkgo's antiplatelet and anti-inflammatory pharmacology.
