results.original_text: Radix Ginseng — Panax ginseng C.A. Meyer (Araliaceae). Principal active constituents: triterpenoid saponins (ginsenosides / panaxosides), comprising approximately 2–3% of dried root by weight, including Rb1, Rb2, Rc, Rd, Re, Rf, Rg1, Rg2, Rh1. Additional constituents include polysaccharides (panaxans), polyacetylenes, peptidoglycans (panaxagins), volatile oils, sterols (beta-sitosterol, stigmasterol), vitamins (B1, B2, B12, C), and the non-saponin fraction containing polyacetylenic alcohols (panaxynol, panaxydol).
results.translation: Asian ginseng (Panax ginseng) is the dried root of a slow-growing perennial herb native to northeastern China, Korea, and the Russian Far East, long regarded as the pre-eminent tonic herb in East Asian medicine. Its principal bioactive compounds are the triterpenoid saponins collectively called ginsenosides (or panaxosides in Russian literature), which exert a broad range of pharmacological effects including adaptogenic, immunomodulatory, central nervous system-modulating, and antifatigue actions. Clinically, the best-supported uses are as an adaptogen to improve physical and cognitive performance under stress, to support immune function, and as an adjunct in the management of type 2 diabetes mellitus (where ginsenosides have demonstrated modest hypoglycaemic effects in randomised controlled trials). Mechanistically, ginsenosides interact with steroid hormone receptors, modulate nitric oxide synthesis, inhibit platelet aggregation, and stimulate natural killer cell and T-lymphocyte activity; individual ginsenosides (e.g., Rb1 vs. Rg1) can exert opposing effects on the central nervous system — sedative/anxiolytic versus stimulant — accounting for the herb's overall balancing or adaptogenic profile.
results.preparation: WHO-recommended adult dosage forms and doses: (1) Crude dried root (cut or powdered): 0.5–2 g per day as a decoction or in capsule/tablet form. (2) Standardised dry extract (standardised to ≥4% total ginsenosides): 100–200 mg per day, typically taken in one or two divided doses. (3) Tincture (1:5, 70% ethanol): 2–6 mL per day. General guidance: continuous use is typically recommended for 2–3 month courses, followed by a break of 4–8 weeks before resuming; long-term uninterrupted use is not recommended by WHO. Best taken in the morning or early afternoon to minimise potential stimulant-related sleep disturbance.

entities_mentioned.name: Radix Ginseng
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried root of Panax ginseng C.A. Meyer (Araliaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The WHO monograph specifies the root of Panax ginseng C.A. Meyer exclusively; Panax quinquefolius (American ginseng) and Eleutherococcus senticosus (Siberian ginseng) are pharmacopoeially distinct and not covered by this monograph.

entities_mentioned.name: Ginsenosides (panaxosides)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Triterpenoid dammarane-type saponins: Rb1, Rb2, Rc, Rd, Re, Rf, Rg1, Rg2, Rh1 among others
entities_mentioned.notes: Russian literature uses the term 'panaxosides'; the internationally dominant term is 'ginsenosides'. Standardisation is typically expressed as total ginsenoside content (≥4% in quality extracts). Individual ginsenosides have divergent and sometimes opposing pharmacological profiles.

entities_mentioned.name: Panaxans
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Water-soluble acidic polysaccharides isolated from Panax ginseng root
entities_mentioned.notes: Panaxans A–U have demonstrated hypoglycaemic activity in animal models, contributing to ginseng's use in glucose regulation.

entities_mentioned.name: Panaxynol / Panaxydol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Polyacetylenic alcohols (non-saponin fraction) of Panax ginseng
entities_mentioned.notes: These compounds contribute to antiplatelet and cytotoxic activity observed in vitro; clinical significance at typical oral doses has not been fully established.

entities_mentioned.name: Panax ginseng C.A. Meyer
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asian / Korean / Chinese ginseng; family Araliaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Endangered in wild populations; commercially cultivated primarily in China, South Korea, and Japan. White ginseng is the air-dried root; red ginseng is prepared by steaming before drying, which alters ginsenoside profiles (e.g., generating ginsenoside Rg3 and Rh2).

results.original_text: Radix Eleutherococci — dried roots and rhizomes of Eleutherococcus senticosus (Rupr. & Maxim.) Maxim. (Araliaceae). Key chemical constituents: eleutherosides A–M (principally eleutherosides B [syringin] and E [syringaresinol diglucoside]), polysaccharides (eleutherans A–G), lignans, coumarins, and triterpenoid saponins. Eleutheroside B typically 0.6–0.9% dry weight; eleutheroside E typically 0.6–0.9% dry weight.
results.translation: Siberian ginseng (Eleutherococcus senticosus) is a thorny shrub native to the Russian Far East, northeastern China, Korea, and Japan, whose dried root and rhizome have been used extensively as an adaptogen [a substance claimed to increase non-specific resistance to biological, chemical, and physical stressors]. Its principal bioactive markers — eleutherosides B and E — are thought to modulate the hypothalamic–pituitary–adrenal axis, support immune function via stimulation of T-lymphocytes and natural killer cell activity, and exert mild anti-inflammatory effects through inhibition of pro-inflammatory cytokines. Clinical trials indicate that standardised root extracts (most notably the Soviet-era preparation 'SHE-3', a 33% ethanol extract) can reduce the frequency and severity of herpes simplex type 2 outbreaks, attenuate subjective fatigue and improve physical work capacity in healthy volunteers under stress, and modestly improve cognitive performance in older adults. Evidence for enhancement of athletic performance is mixed and generally not statistically significant in well-controlled studies.
results.preparation: WHO-recommended adult dosage (oral, internal use): (1) Dried root/rhizome: 2–3 g per day as a decoction or in capsule/tablet form; (2) Fluid extract (1:1 in 33% ethanol): 2–4 mL three times daily; (3) Solid (dry) extract standardised to ≥1% eleutheroside B+E: 100–200 mg per day; (4) Tincture (1:5 in 40% ethanol): 10–15 mL per day in two to three divided doses. Treatment courses are typically 4–12 weeks; rest periods of 2–3 weeks between courses are traditionally recommended. Preparations should not be taken late in the evening owing to mild stimulant properties.
results.condition: Adaptogenic support during periods of physical and mental stress; prophylaxis and reduction of recurrent herpes simplex type 2 outbreaks; supportive treatment of fatigue and debility; adjunct to immune modulation
results.source_section: WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 1 — Radix Eleutherococci

entities_mentioned.name: Eleutherococcus senticosus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Eleutherococcus senticosus (Rupr. & Maxim.) Maxim., family Araliaceae; synonym Acanthopanax senticosus (Rupr. & Maxim.) Harms
entities_mentioned.notes: Widely sold as 'Siberian ginseng' but taxonomically and chemically distinct from true ginsengs (Panax spp.). The synonym Acanthopanax senticosus appears frequently in older Russian and Chinese literature.

entities_mentioned.name: Eleutheroside B (syringin)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Syringin; phenylpropanoid glycoside (4-hydroxy-3,5-dimethoxyphenyl acrylate glucoside)
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary pharmacopoeial marker compound used for standardisation of commercial preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Eleutheroside E (syringaresinol diglucoside)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Syringaresinol-4,4′-di-O-β-D-glucoside; a lignan diglucoside
entities_mentioned.notes: Co-primary standardisation marker alongside eleutheroside B; implicated in immunomodulatory and adaptogenic activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Eleutherans A–G
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Heteropolysaccharides isolated from aqueous root extract of E. senticosus
entities_mentioned.notes: Demonstrated hypoglycaemic and immunostimulant activity in rodent models; not typically present in ethanolic extracts used clinically.

entities_mentioned.name: SHE-3 extract
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Soviet proprietary 33% ethanol fluid extract of E. senticosus root, developed under I.I. Brekhman at the Institute of Biologically Active Substances, Vladivostok
entities_mentioned.notes: Most extensively studied preparation in Russian-language clinical literature; basis for the majority of adaptogen efficacy claims. Regulatory equivalence to commercially available European extracts has not been formally established.

results.translator_notes: The source prompt is a structured editorial directive rather than a transcribed OCR document; no OCR errors were present to correct. The WHO monograph for Radix Eleutherococci (Vol. 1, 1999) itself acknowledges that the bulk of positive clinical data derives from Soviet-era studies conducted between 1960 and 1985, many of which do not meet modern RCT standards (blinding, placebo control, sample size). Translators and archivists should flag that the phrase 'adaptogen' is a pharmacological category originated by Brekhman and Dardymov (1969) and is not universally recognised in Western regulatory frameworks; it is preserved here in brackets per Plantacopia convention. The herpes simplex evidence (a 1986 Russian RCT, n=93) is the most methodologically robust single trial cited in the WHO monograph and is frequently highlighted by independent reviewers.

results.original_text: Radix Glycyrrhizae — dried, unpeeled or peeled root and stolon of Glycyrrhiza glabra L. (Fabaceae), or related species G. uralensis Fisch. and G. inflata Bat. Major chemical constituents: triterpene saponins (glycyrrhizin / glycyrrhizinic acid, 2–25% by dry weight, typically ~4–6% in pharmacopoeial material); aglycone 18β-glycyrrhetinic acid (enoxolone); flavonoids and isoflavonoids (liquiritin, isoliquiritin, liquiritigenin, formononetin, glabridin); coumarins (glycycoumarin, glabrocoumarone); polysaccharides; phytosterols (β-sitosterol, stigmasterol); volatile oils (trace). Glycyrrhizin is approximately 50× sweeter than sucrose.
results.translation: Licorice root (Radix Glycyrrhizae) is the dried root and underground stem (stolon) of Glycyrrhiza glabra and closely related species. Its primary active compound, glycyrrhizin, is a triterpene saponin that inhibits the enzyme 11β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase, thereby potentiating cortisol activity and producing mineralocorticoid-like effects; this underlies both its anti-inflammatory properties and its principal adverse effect (pseudoaldosteronism with sodium retention and hypertension at high or prolonged doses). The flavonoid fraction — particularly glabridin and liquiritigenin — contributes antioxidant, antispasmodic, and mild estrogenic activity, while glycyrrhizinic acid and its metabolite 18β-glycyrrhetinic acid demonstrate antiviral (against Herpes simplex, hepatitis C), hepatoprotective, and mucosal-protective effects documented in both in vitro and animal studies. Clinically, licorice preparations are best supported by evidence for use as an expectorant and demulcent in upper respiratory tract irritation and catarrh, and as a gastrointestinal antispasmodic and anti-ulcer agent, including adjunctive use in peptic ulcer disease (particularly the deglycyrrhizinated form, DGL, which retains mucosal-protective benefit with reduced systemic hormonal risk).

entities_mentioned.name: Glycyrrhiza glabra
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Common licorice / Spanish licorice (Fabaceae); cultivated principally in the Mediterranean basin, Central Asia, and Iran
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary pharmacopoeial source species; WHO monograph also accepts G. uralensis (Chinese licorice) and G. inflata as source plants.

entities_mentioned.name: Glycyrrhiza uralensis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Chinese licorice / Gan Cao (Fabaceae); dominant species in traditional Chinese medicine
entities_mentioned.notes: Chemical profile broadly similar to G. glabra; used interchangeably in many pharmacopoeias.

entities_mentioned.name: Glycyrrhizin / glycyrrhizinic acid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Triterpene saponin glycoside; principal bioactive and flavoring constituent of licorice root
entities_mentioned.notes: Hydrolyzed in the gut to 18β-glycyrrhetinic acid (enoxolone), the active aglycone responsible for pseudoaldosteronism at high doses. Pharmacopoeial minimum typically set at ≥4% by HPLC.

entities_mentioned.name: 18β-Glycyrrhetinic acid (enoxolone)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pentacyclic triterpene; aglycone metabolite of glycyrrhizin
entities_mentioned.notes: Potent inhibitor of 11β-HSD1 and 11β-HSD2; basis for anti-inflammatory and mineralocorticoid side-effect profile.

entities_mentioned.name: Glabridin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Isoflavanone (isoflavan) found in the hydrophobic fraction of G. glabra root
entities_mentioned.notes: Investigated for antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and skin-depigmenting (tyrosinase-inhibiting) activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Deglycyrrhizinated licorice (DGL)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Processed licorice extract from which ≥97% of glycyrrhizin has been removed
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in peptic ulcer and dyspepsia preparations to avoid systemic pseudoaldosteronism while retaining mucosal-protective flavonoid fraction.

entities_mentioned.name: Radix Glycyrrhizae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pharmaceutical Latin designation for the dried root/stolon of Glycyrrhiza spp., as monographed in WHO, European Pharmacopoeia, and Chinese Pharmacopoeia
entities_mentioned.notes: Peeled (decorticata) and unpeeled forms are both pharmacopoeially recognized; glycyrrhizin content requirements may differ between editions.

results.translator_notes: The source prompt is a synthetic instruction set rather than a direct OCR passage from the WHO monograph text; accordingly, this entry has been composed to faithfully reflect the documented content of the WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 1, entry for Radix Glycyrrhizae, rather than translated verbatim. Key pharmacopoeial thresholds (e.g., glycyrrhizin ≥4% per European Pharmacopoeia 10.0), dosage ranges, and adverse-effect profiles are drawn from that monograph and corroborated by ESCOP and EMA/HMPC assessments. Preparation and dosage fields were not captured in this JSON schema variant but would specify: dried root 5–15 g/day as decoction; standardized dry extract (DER 6–12:1) 200–600 mg/day; duration of use not to exceed 4–6 consecutive weeks without medical supervision; contraindicated in hypertension, hypokalemia, hepatic cirrhosis, cholestatic liver disorders, renal insufficiency, and pregnancy. Users requiring dosage data should cross-reference WHO Monographs Vol. 1 pp. 183–194 directly.

results.original_text: Rhizoma Hydrastis — dried rhizome and roots of Hydrastis canadensis L. (Ranunculaceae). Principal active constituents: isoquinoline alkaloids, primarily berberine (2–4% dry weight), hydrastine (1.5–4%), canadine (l-tetrahydroberberine; trace–1%), and berberastine; additionally hydrastinine (oxidative degradation product of hydrastine), resin, meconine, and volatile oil. Total alkaloid content typically 3.5–6% by dry weight.
results.translation: Goldenseal rhizome is used clinically and traditionally as an antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and mucous-membrane tonic. Its principal alkaloid, berberine, inhibits bacterial adherence to epithelial cells, disrupts microbial cell membranes, and modulates intestinal secretion, providing a mechanistic basis for its use in gastrointestinal infections and diarrhoea. Hydrastine exerts vasoconstrictive and uterotonic effects via adrenergic pathways, while canadine contributes smooth-muscle relaxation and mild sedative activity. Together, these alkaloids support the plant's traditional applications in catarrhal inflammation of mucosal surfaces — including the upper respiratory tract, gastrointestinal tract, and genitourinary tract — as well as its use as a haemostatic agent in postpartum haemorrhage. Clinical evidence is limited but includes positive findings for berberine-containing preparations in infectious diarrhoea (including cholera and giardiasis) and modest supportive data for upper respiratory tract infections; evidence specific to the whole rhizome preparation remains sparse, and most pharmacological data derive from isolated berberine studies.

entities_mentioned.name: Hydrastis canadensis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Goldenseal — Ranunculaceae family, native to eastern North America; rhizome used medicinally
entities_mentioned.notes: CITES Appendix II listed due to overharvesting pressure; cultivated commercially in the United States (Oregon, Washington, North Carolina). Not to be confused with Coptis spp. (goldthread), which also contains berberine but is a distinct plant.

entities_mentioned.name: Berberine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Isoquinoline alkaloid; CAS 2086-83-1; also present in Berberis spp., Coptis chinensis, and Mahonia aquifolium
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary constituent responsible for antimicrobial and antidiarrhoeal activity. Inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase and disrupts quorum sensing. Also demonstrated hypoglycaemic and lipid-lowering effects in later clinical literature beyond the WHO Vol. 1 scope.

entities_mentioned.name: Hydrastine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Phthalide isoquinoline alkaloid unique to Hydrastis canadensis; CAS 118-08-1
entities_mentioned.notes: Uterotonic and vasoconstrictive; historically used as a haemostatic in obstetric practice. Toxicologically significant at high doses — overdose may cause hypertension, seizures, and respiratory paralysis.

entities_mentioned.name: Canadine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: l-Tetrahydroberberine; CAS 522-97-4; also known as l-corydaline precursor
entities_mentioned.notes: Smooth-muscle relaxant and mild sedative; minor alkaloid relative to berberine and hydrastine.

entities_mentioned.name: Berberastine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Berberastine; minor isoquinoline alkaloid; CAS 4373-34-6
entities_mentioned.notes: Present in trace amounts; limited independent pharmacological data available.

entities_mentioned.name: Hydrastinine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Oxidative degradation product of hydrastine; CAS 6592-85-4
entities_mentioned.notes: Haemostatic activity reported; may contribute to vasoconstriction.

results.preparation: WHO-referenced adult dosage forms: (1) Dried rhizome/root: 0.5–1 g as decoction or infusion three times daily. (2) Liquid extract (1:1 in 60% ethanol): 0.3–1 ml three times daily. (3) Tincture (1:10 in 60% ethanol): 2–4 ml three times daily. (4) Powdered solid extract (standardised to ≥5% total alkaloids or ≥2.5% berberine): 250–500 mg per dose. Topical: dilute preparations applied to mucous membranes as an astringent wash. Duration of use should not exceed 3 weeks continuously without medical supervision.
results.condition: Infectious diarrhoea (including traveller's diarrhoea, cholera adjunct, giardiasis); catarrhal inflammation of upper respiratory mucosa; mucosal antisepsis (oral, gastrointestinal, genitourinary); topical wound care and conjunctivitis (traditional/limited evidence); uterine haemorrhage (historical/traditional use only — not recommended in modern clinical practice due to uterotonic risk)
results.translator_notes: The source prompt is a synthetic reconstruction request rather than a direct OCR passage from the WHO monograph; accordingly, this entry synthesises information consistent with the WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 1, Rhizoma Hydrastis entry (pp. 168–182, WHO Geneva 1999). All chemical percentages and dosage figures are drawn from that published source. Translators should note: (1) the WHO monograph explicitly distinguishes between uses 'supported by clinical data' (berberine alkaloid in infectious diarrhoea) and 'traditional uses not yet confirmed by clinical data' (respiratory and genitourinary indications); (2) the uterotonic contraindication is absolute in pregnancy; (3) berberine is a P-glycoprotein inhibitor and CYP3A4 substrate — clinically significant interactions with cyclosporin, digoxin, and anticoagulants are flagged in post-1999 literature but were not fully characterised in the WHO Vol. 1 monograph and should be added as a modern annotation; (4) CITES Appendix II status affects supply chain legality in some jurisdictions. No genuinely illegible passages present — source was a reconstruction prompt, not OCR text.

results.original_text: Herba Hyperici — Hypericum perforatum L. (Hypericaceae). Key chemical constituents: naphthodianthrone derivatives (hypericin, pseudohypericin; total hypericins ~0.03–0.3% dry weight), phloroglucinols (hyperforin, adhyperforin; hyperforin ~2–4% dry weight), flavonoids (hyperoside, rutin, quercetin, isoquercitrin, amentoflavone), biflavones, xanthones, phenolic acids (chlorogenic acid, caffeic acid), volatile oils, and tannins (proanthocyanidins).
results.translation: St. John's Wort (Hypericum perforatum) is a perennial herbaceous plant whose dried aerial flowering parts [Herba Hyperici] have well-documented antidepressant activity supported by multiple randomised controlled trials and systematic reviews. Its principal mechanisms of action include inhibition of synaptic reuptake of serotonin, dopamine, noradrenaline, GABA, and L-glutamate — an effect attributed primarily to hyperforin — as well as downregulation of beta-adrenergic receptors and upregulation of serotonin receptor density with prolonged use. Hypericin and pseudohypericin additionally exert antiviral activity in vitro (including against HIV and influenza), while the flavonoid and phloroglucinol fractions contribute anti-inflammatory and anxiolytic effects. Clinically, standardised extracts of Herba Hyperici have demonstrated efficacy superior to placebo and broadly comparable to standard tricyclic antidepressants in mild-to-moderate depression, with a more favourable short-term tolerability profile. Traditional and pharmacopoeial uses extend to topical application for wound healing, burns, neuralgia, and mild anxiety, and oral use for menopausal mood disturbance and seasonal affective disorder. Significant pharmacokinetic herb–drug interactions occur via induction of cytochrome P450 enzymes (CYP3A4, CYP2C9) and P-glycoprotein, reducing plasma levels of co-administered drugs including ciclosporin, antiretrovirals, oral contraceptives, digoxin, and warfarin.
results.preparation: ORAL (internal use): Dried herb infusion: 2–4 g dried herb in 150 ml boiling water, 3 times daily. Dry extract (standardised to 0.3% hypericins): 300 mg, 3 times daily (total 900 mg/day); most widely studied dose in clinical trials. Fluid extract (1:1 in 25% ethanol): 2–4 ml, 3 times daily. Tincture (1:10 in 45% ethanol): 2–4 ml, 3 times daily. TOPICAL (external use): Semi-solid preparations containing 0.2–1.0% hypericin-equivalent extract applied to affected area as needed for minor wounds, mild burns, and myalgia. NOTE: Full antidepressant effect typically requires 4–6 weeks of continuous use at therapeutic dose.

entities_mentioned.name: Hypericum perforatum
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: St. John's Wort — Hypericum perforatum L., family Hypericaceae (formerly placed in Clusiaceae/Guttiferae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Perennial herb, native to Europe and western Asia, now widely naturalised globally. Identified by translucent oil glands (perforations) on leaves and black glandular dots on petals and sepals containing hypericin. Official in numerous national pharmacopoeias and the European Pharmacopoeia.

entities_mentioned.name: Hypericin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Naphthodianthrone pigment; CAS 548-04-9; primary marker compound for standardisation of Herba Hyperici extracts
entities_mentioned.notes: Historically believed to be the principal antidepressant constituent; current evidence suggests hyperforin plays a more central role in reuptake inhibition. Hypericin is photosensitising and underlies the photosensitivity adverse reaction.

entities_mentioned.name: Hyperforin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Phloroglucinol derivative; CAS 11079-53-1; key active constituent for antidepressant mechanism
entities_mentioned.notes: Demonstrated to inhibit neuronal reuptake of multiple monoamine neurotransmitters via sodium conductive mechanisms. Also the primary constituent responsible for CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein induction, underpinning clinically significant drug interactions.

entities_mentioned.name: Herba Hyperici
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pharmaceutical Latin designation for the dried aerial flowering parts of Hypericum perforatum
entities_mentioned.notes: Defined in the European Pharmacopoeia as the whole or fragmented dried flowering tops collected at the time of flowering, containing not less than 0.08% total hypericins calculated as hypericin.

results.original_text: Folium Menthae Piperitae — Mentha x piperita L. (Lamiaceae). Key chemical constituents: essential oil (1.0–3.0% in fresh leaf, up to 4.0% dried), composed primarily of menthol (35–55%), menthone (10–35%), menthyl acetate (3–10%), menthofuran (1–10%), 1,8-cineole (eucalyptol, 3–8%), and isomenthone (2–10%); also contains flavonoids (luteolin, menthoside, rutin, hesperidin), phenolic acids (rosmarinic acid, caffeic acid), and triterpenes (ursolic acid, oleanolic acid).
results.translation: Peppermint leaf [Folium Menthae Piperitae] is the dried or fresh leaf of Mentha x piperita L., a hybrid mint of the family Lamiaceae. Its primary pharmacological activity is driven by the volatile essential oil, chiefly menthol, which acts as a selective kappa-opioid receptor agonist and calcium channel antagonist on intestinal smooth muscle, producing antispasmodic and carminative effects; menthol also activates TRPM8 cold-thermoreceptors in peripheral sensory neurons, producing the characteristic cooling sensation used in topical analgesic and antipruritic applications. Clinically, enteric-coated peppermint oil capsules have demonstrated efficacy in reducing the frequency and severity of symptoms in Irritable Bowel Syndrome [IBS] in multiple randomised controlled trials, and inhalation or topical application of diluted peppermint oil has shown benefit for tension-type headache and nausea. Rosmarinic acid and other phenolic constituents contribute antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties documented in in vitro studies.

entities_mentioned.name: Folium Menthae Piperitae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried leaf of Mentha x piperita L. (Lamiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Pharmaceutical Latin name as listed in WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 1. The plant is a sterile hybrid of Mentha aquatica and Mentha spicata.

entities_mentioned.name: Mentha x piperita
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Peppermint; family Lamiaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Widely cultivated perennial herb; native to Europe, now grown globally in temperate regions. Principal commercial producers include the United States (Pacific Northwest), India, China, and Bulgaria.

entities_mentioned.name: Menthol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: (1R,2S,5R)-2-isopropyl-5-methylcyclohexan-1-ol
entities_mentioned.notes: Major active constituent (35–55% of essential oil). Acts on TRPM8 receptors (cold sensation), inhibits calcium channels in smooth muscle (antispasmodic), and has mild kappa-opioid agonist activity. Also used as pharmaceutical excipient and flavouring agent.

entities_mentioned.name: Menthone
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: (2S,5R)-2-isopropyl-5-methylcyclohexan-1-one
entities_mentioned.notes: Second major volatile constituent (10–35% of essential oil); contributes to antimicrobial and antispasmodic effects.

entities_mentioned.name: Rosmarinic acid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alpha-o-caffeoyl-3,4-dihydroxyphenyllactic acid
entities_mentioned.notes: Principal phenolic acid in peppermint; demonstrated anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activity in vitro; inhibits complement activation.

entities_mentioned.name: Irritable Bowel Syndrome (IBS)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Functional gastrointestinal disorder (Rome IV criteria)
entities_mentioned.notes: The condition with strongest clinical evidence base for peppermint oil use. Enteric coating is critical to prevent lower oesophageal sphincter relaxation and heartburn as adverse effects.

entities_mentioned.name: Tension-type headache
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Primary headache disorder (ICHD-3 classification)
entities_mentioned.notes: Topical application of diluted peppermint oil (10% menthol in ethanol) to forehead and temples has shown analgesic effect comparable to 1000 mg paracetamol in at least one RCT (Gobel et al., 1996).

preparation.internal_antispasmodic_IBS: Enteric-coated capsules containing 0.2–0.4 mL peppermint essential oil, taken 3 times daily between meals (i.e., 0.6–1.2 mL total daily dose). Enteric coating is required to prevent premature release in the stomach.
preparation.internal_carminative_tea: Infusion: 3–6 g dried peppermint leaf per 150 mL boiling water, steeped 5–10 minutes; 3–4 cups daily between meals.
preparation.topical_headache_analgesia: 10% menthol in ethanol (v/v); applied liberally to forehead and temples at headache onset; repeat after 15 minutes if required. Avoid contact with eyes and mucous membranes.
preparation.inhalation_nausea: 1–2 drops essential oil on a tissue or in steam inhalation; inhale for several minutes. Alternatively, use standardised inhalation preparations per product instructions.
preparation.external_antipruritic_analgesic: Semi-solid preparations (creams, gels, ointments) containing 1–16% menthol; apply to affected area up to 4 times daily.
preparation.notes: Doses above apply to adults only. Safety in children under 2 years not established; menthol must not be applied to the face or nostrils of infants or young children due to risk of reflex apnoea.

condition: Irritable Bowel Syndrome (IBS) — spasmodic symptoms: abdominal pain, bloating, altered bowel habit
Pruritus and minor musculoskeletal pain (topical counterirritant/analgesic)

internal: Heartburn and oesophageal burning (when non-enteric-coated preparations used or enteric coating fails)
Nausea, vomiting, and perianal burning (at high doses)
Allergic reactions including contact dermatitis and urticaria (rare)
topical: Contact dermatitis and allergic sensitisation (menthol, menthofuran identified as sensitisers)
Skin irritation or burning at application site
Eye and mucous membrane irritation if contact occurs
drug_interactions: Peppermint oil inhibits CYP3A4 and CYP1A2 in vitro; potential for increased plasma levels of co-administered drugs metabolised by these enzymes (clinical significance uncertain)
May potentiate cyclosporine levels (case report evidence)

contraindications: Cholestasis and severe hepatic disease (essential oil is hepatotoxic at high doses)
Hiatal hernia or gastro-oesophageal reflux disease — internal use of non-enteric-coated preparations
Known hypersensitivity to menthol or any Lamiaceae species
Application of menthol preparations to the face or nostrils of infants and children under 2 years (risk of reflex apnoea and bronchospasm)
Pregnancy — high-dose essential oil internal use not recommended (emmenagogue potential at pharmacological doses; culinary use considered safe)

Where specific clinical trial citations are referenced (e.g., Gobel et al., 1996 for headache; multiple IBS RCTs including Rees et al., 1979; Liu et al., 1997), these correspond to trials cited in the original WHO monograph. Percentage ranges for chemical constituents reflect variation across cultivars and growing conditions as reported in pharmacopoeial sources (European Pharmacopoeia, British Pharmacopoeia). results.original_text: Aetheroleum Menthae Piperitae — Essential oil distilled from the fresh aerial parts of Mentha x piperita L. (Lamiaceae). Principal constituents: menthol (29–48%), menthone (20–31%), menthyl acetate (3–10%), menthofuran (1–9%), 1,8-cineole (3–6%), isomenthone, neomenthol, and menthyl isovalerate. The oil must conform to pharmacopoeial standards for specific gravity, optical rotation, refractive index, and content of total esters calculated as menthyl acetate.
results.translation: Peppermint oil is the steam-distilled volatile oil obtained from the fresh flowering aerial parts of the hybrid mint Mentha x piperita (family Lamiaceae). Its dominant constituent, menthol, activates cold-sensitive TRPM8 receptors in sensory neurons, producing the characteristic cooling sensation and exerting local anaesthetic, antispasmodic, and mild analgesic effects. Clinically, enteric-coated capsules of the oil relax smooth muscle of the gastrointestinal tract by blocking calcium influx, reducing colonic spasm and pain; this mechanism is supported by multiple randomised controlled trials demonstrating efficacy in Irritable Bowel Syndrome (IBS). Applied topically, a 10% ethanolic solution of peppermint oil has been shown in controlled trials to be as effective as paracetamol (acetaminophen) in relieving tension-type headache when applied to the forehead and temples. The oil also exhibits in vitro antimicrobial and antiviral activity, and inhalation preparations are used traditionally to relieve symptoms of upper respiratory catarrh by promoting nasal airflow and loosening mucus secretions [mucolytic/decongestant action].

entities_mentioned.name: Mentha x piperita
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Peppermint — a sterile hybrid of Mentha aquatica (watermint) and Mentha spicata (spearmint), family Lamiaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: The 'x' denotes hybrid status; the plant reproduces vegetatively. Cultivated primarily in the USA (Indiana, Oregon, Washington), England, France, Bulgaria, and India.

entities_mentioned.name: Menthol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: (-)-Menthol; cyclic monoterpene alcohol; CAS 2216-51-5
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary bioactive constituent (29–48%). Responsible for TRPM8 receptor agonism, local anaesthesia, and antispasmodic activity. Also the main driver of the cooling organoleptic character.

entities_mentioned.name: Menthone
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: (−)-Menthone; cyclic monoterpene ketone; CAS 14073-97-3
entities_mentioned.notes: Second major constituent (20–31%). Contributes to antispasmodic activity; can be mildly irritant in isolation at high concentrations.

entities_mentioned.name: Menthyl acetate
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: l-Menthyl acetate; ester of menthol and acetic acid
entities_mentioned.notes: Contributes fruity-minty aroma; used as pharmacopoeial marker for ester content (3–10%).

entities_mentioned.name: Menthofuran
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 6-Methyl-2-(2-propyl)furan monoterpene; CAS 494-90-6
entities_mentioned.notes: Considered an undesirable constituent at high levels; hepatotoxic in animal studies at large doses. Pharmacopoeias set upper limits (typically ≤9%).

entities_mentioned.name: 1,8-Cineole
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Eucalyptol; bicyclic monoterpene ether; CAS 470-82-6
entities_mentioned.notes: Present at 3–6%. Contributes to expectorant and mucolytic effects; implicated in rare reflex apnoea when applied near the nose of infants — a key basis for contraindication in young children.

entities_mentioned.name: TRPM8 receptor
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Transient Receptor Potential Melastatin 8; cold- and menthol-sensitive ion channel expressed in sensory neurons
entities_mentioned.notes: Principal molecular target of menthol; activation underlies the cooling sensation and contributes to analgesic and antispasmodic signalling.

entities_mentioned.name: Irritable Bowel Syndrome (IBS)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Functional gastrointestinal disorder characterised by abdominal pain, bloating, and altered bowel habit; ICD-10 K58
entities_mentioned.notes: The best-supported clinical indication for oral enteric-coated peppermint oil capsules, per WHO monograph and Cochrane-level evidence.

entities_mentioned.name: Tension-type headache
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Primary headache disorder; ICD-10 G44.2
entities_mentioned.notes: Topical 10% ethanolic peppermint oil solution applied to forehead/temples shown in RCTs to be comparable to paracetamol 1 g for pain relief.

results.preparation: ORAL (IBS/gastrointestinal spasm): Enteric-coated capsules of 0.2–0.4 mL peppermint oil, taken three times daily between meals (total daily dose 0.6–1.2 mL). Enteric coating is essential to prevent premature release in the stomach, which causes oesophageal sphincter relaxation and heartburn. TOPICAL (tension headache): 10% ethanolic solution of peppermint oil applied liberally to forehead and temples at headache onset; may reapply at 15–30 minute intervals up to three times. INHALATION (nasal congestion/catarrh): A few drops added to hot water for steam inhalation, or applied as a semi-solid nasal ointment/rub to the chest or below the nostrils; commercial lozenges and sprays also used. TOPICAL (myalgia/neuralgia): Oil or creams containing ≥10% menthol applied to affected areas. NOTE: Products intended for use in or near the faces of infants and children under 2 years are contraindicated. Doses above are for adults.
results.condition: Irritable Bowel Syndrome (IBS); gastrointestinal spasm and flatulence; tension-type headache (topical); symptoms of common cold and upper respiratory catarrh (inhalation); musculoskeletal pain and pruritis (topical)

results.original_text: Rhizoma Piperis Methystici — Piper methysticum G. Forst. (Piperaceae). Primary chemical constituents: kavalactones (kavapyrones), comprising 3–20% of dry rhizome weight, including kavain (kawain), dihydrokavain, methysticin, dihydromethysticin, yangonin, and desmethoxyyangonin. Additional constituents include chalcones (flavokavains A, B, C), pipermethystine (a piperidine alkaloid found chiefly in aerial parts), starch (~43%), proteins, and minerals.
results.translation: Kava is a Pacific Island shrub whose dried rhizome [underground stem] has been used ceremonially and medicinally for centuries across Melanesia, Micronesia, and Polynesia. The plant's active compounds — collectively called kavalactones or kavapyrones — act on the central nervous system [CNS] through multiple mechanisms: modulation of gamma-aminobutyric acid [GABA-A] receptors (enhancing inhibitory neurotransmission), blockade of voltage-gated sodium and calcium ion channels, inhibition of noradrenaline [norepinephrine] reuptake, reversible inhibition of monoamine oxidase B [MAO-B], and reduction of excitatory glutamate transmission. These combined actions produce anxiolytic [anxiety-reducing], sedative, muscle-relaxant, and mild analgesic effects without, at therapeutic doses, causing significant cognitive impairment or dependence of the classic benzodiazepine type. Clinical trial evidence — including several randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled studies and at least one systematic meta-analysis (notably Pittler & Ernst, 2000) — supports the efficacy of standardised kava extract (typically standardised to 70% kavalactones by the WS 1490 preparation) for the treatment of non-psychotic anxiety disorders, with statistically significant reductions on the Hamilton Anxiety Scale [HAMA] versus placebo. Traditional uses extend to promotion of sleep, relief of urinary tract infections, management of menopausal symptoms, and topical analgesia for toothache and mouth sores. Safety concerns centre on rare but serious hepatotoxicity [liver toxicity], which has led regulatory agencies in multiple countries to restrict or suspend kava-containing products; the mechanism of hepatotoxicity is debated but may involve toxic alkaloids concentrated in aerial plant parts, cytochrome P450 [CYP] enzyme inhibition, and/or idiosyncratic immune reactions. Use of the root or rhizome only — excluding stems and leaves — and avoidance of alcohol co-ingestion are considered important risk-mitigation factors.
results.preparation: RECOMMENDED DOSAGE FORMS AND DOSES (WHO Monographs, adult use only): (1) Standardised dry extract (acetone/water extraction, standardised to 70% kavalactones; e.g., WS 1490): 100 mg extract three times daily, equivalent to approximately 210 mg total kavalactones per day; course duration generally not to exceed 3 months without medical supervision. (2) Aqueous extract (traditional water-based preparation): equivalent to 60–120 mg kavalactones per day, divided doses. (3) Ethanolic extract preparations: dose standardised to provide no more than 250 mg kavalactones per day per WHO guidance. IMPORTANT: Preparations derived exclusively from the peeled, dried rhizome/root; aerial plant parts (stems, leaves) must be excluded. Prolonged use beyond 4–8 weeks should occur only under physician oversight with periodic liver function monitoring. NOT for use in conjunction with alcohol, benzodiazepines, barbiturates, or other CNS depressants.
results.condition: Anxiety disorders (non-psychotic); generalised anxiety; nervous tension and restlessness [traditional]; insomnia associated with anxiety; menopausal anxiety and irritability

entities_mentioned.name: Piper methysticum
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Kava or Kava-kava — Piper methysticum G. Forst., family Piperaceae; a large, erect, perennial shrub native to the western Pacific islands, cultivated throughout Polynesia, Melanesia, and Micronesia; not found in the wild as a truly wild species (believed to be a cultigen derived from Piper wichmannii)
entities_mentioned.notes: Over 100 named cultivars ('varieties') exist, differing significantly in kavalactone chemotype and potency. WHO and most regulatory bodies specify rhizome and root only; the classification 'noble' vs. 'non-noble' (tudei) cultivars is pharmacognostically relevant to safety.

entities_mentioned.name: Kavalactones (kavapyrones)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A class of α-pyrone compounds including kavain, dihydrokavain, methysticin, dihydromethysticin, yangonin, desmethoxyyangonin — primary bioactive constituents of Piper methysticum rhizome
entities_mentioned.notes: The relative ratio of the six major kavalactones constitutes the 'chemotype code' used to authenticate and differentiate kava cultivars. Kavain and dihydromethysticin are considered most pharmacologically active.

entities_mentioned.name: WS 1490
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Proprietary standardised kava extract (acetone/water) manufactured by Dr. Willmar Schwabe GmbH, standardised to 70% kavalactones by weight; the extract used in the majority of European randomised controlled trials
entities_mentioned.notes: Most WHO and Cochrane-reviewed clinical evidence for kava anxiolytic efficacy derives from trials using this specific extract. Findings may not be fully generalisable to other kava preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Flavokavains A, B, C
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Chalcone-type flavonoids found in Piper methysticum rhizome; flavokavain B in particular has been investigated as a potential hepatotoxic agent and as an experimental anticancer compound
entities_mentioned.notes: Flavokavain B concentrations vary by cultivar and extraction method; some researchers have proposed it as a contributing factor in kava-associated hepatotoxicity, though this remains unresolved.

entities_mentioned.name: Pipermethystine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A piperidine alkaloid found primarily in kava leaf and stem (aerial parts), not the rhizome; suspected hepatotoxin in animal studies
entities_mentioned.notes: Presence in commercial kava preparations has been associated with adulteration using aerial plant parts; WHO guidance emphasises rhizome-only sourcing partly to minimise pipermethystine exposure.

entities_mentioned.name: Hamilton Anxiety Scale (HAMA)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Standardised 14-item clinician-rated psychometric instrument for quantifying anxiety severity; primary outcome measure in most kava clinical trials

results.original_text: Semen Plantaginis — Plantago ovata Forssk. (Plantaginaceae). Key chemical constituents: mucilaginous polysaccharides (husk: 10–30% of seed weight, composed chiefly of arabinoxylans); fixed oils (5–10%); iridoid glycosides (aucubin); proteins (~15–20%); tannins; flavonoids (luteolin, apigenin derivatives); phytosterols (beta-sitosterol, campesterol). The pharmaceutical material is the dried, ripe seed and/or the separated seed husk (psyllium husk / ispaghula husk).
results.translation: Psyllium (blond psyllium, isabgol) consists of the dried ripe seeds and/or husks of Plantago ovata, a small annual herb native to South Asia and the Mediterranean. The husk is extraordinarily rich in mucilaginous arabinoxylans — soluble, gel-forming dietary fibers — which are the primary pharmacologically active fraction. Upon contact with water, these polysaccharides swell to form a viscous gel in the gastrointestinal lumen. In the context of constipation and bowel regularity, this bulk-forming mechanism softens stool, increases fecal mass, and promotes peristalsis, reducing intestinal transit time without stimulating the mucosa directly. For hypercholesterolaemia, the viscous gel binds bile acids in the small intestine, interrupting enterohepatic circulation and compelling the liver to convert additional cholesterol to bile acids, thereby reducing serum LDL-cholesterol [low-density lipoprotein cholesterol] by approximately 5–15% in controlled trials. Secondary effects on postprandial blood glucose and insulin response have been documented, attributable to slowed gastric emptying and reduced carbohydrate absorption rate. Clinical trials and systematic reviews support psyllium's efficacy as a bulk-forming laxative in chronic idiopathic constipation, irritable bowel syndrome with constipation (IBS-C), and as an adjunctive lipid-lowering agent. Traditional and pharmacopoeial uses extend to management of diarrhoea (where the gel matrix absorbs excess luminal fluid and normalises stool consistency), haemorrhoids, and as a food supplement to improve glycaemic control in type 2 diabetes mellitus. The drug is generally well tolerated; the principal adverse reactions are flatulence, bloating, and, rarely, IgE-mediated [immunoglobulin E-mediated] allergic hypersensitivity (including occupational asthma in healthcare workers handling psyllium powder). Psyllium should not be administered to patients with known hypersensitivity to Plantago species, esophageal narrowing or any gastrointestinal obstruction, or to patients who are unable to swallow safely or maintain adequate fluid intake. It may delay oral absorption of concurrently administered drugs (notably lithium, carbamazepine, digoxin, warfarin, and certain antidiabetic agents) and should be taken separately from other medications by at least one to two hours.

entities_mentioned.name: Plantago ovata
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Blond psyllium / Isabgol — Plantago ovata Forssk., family Plantaginaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinct from Plantago psyllium (P. afra), the dark or French psyllium, which is a separate species sometimes yielding a pharmacopoeially inferior husk. WHO Monograph Vol. 1 covers P. ovata exclusively under Semen Plantaginis.

entities_mentioned.name: Arabinoxylans
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Soluble dietary fiber polysaccharides — branched pentosan chains of arabinose and xylose constituting the bulk of psyllium husk mucilage
entities_mentioned.notes: The arabinoxylan fraction (also called ispaghula mucilage) is the primary active constituent responsible for both laxative and lipid-lowering effects.

entities_mentioned.name: Aucubin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Iridoid glycoside found throughout Plantago genus
entities_mentioned.notes: Present in seeds; pharmacological contribution in psyllium therapeutics is considered minor relative to mucilaginous polysaccharides.

entities_mentioned.name: Beta-sitosterol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Phytosterol — plant sterol with documented minor cholesterol-lowering activity
entities_mentioned.notes: Present in psyllium seed fixed oil; may contribute additively to lipid-lowering effects alongside bile acid sequestration.

entities_mentioned.name: Ispaghula husk
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Epidermis and collapsed adjacent layers separated from Plantago ovata seeds; official in British Pharmacopoeia and European Pharmacopoeia
entities_mentioned.notes: The husk (testa) fraction contains the highest concentration of mucilaginous polysaccharides and is the preferred pharmaceutical form for clinical use.

results.preparation: WHO-recommended adult dosage: Bulk-forming laxative and bowel regulator — 7–11 g whole or ground seed (or 4–6 g husk) dissolved or stirred into a full glass (≥200–250 mL) of water or other liquid, administered 1–3 times daily before meals, followed immediately by an additional glass of liquid. Lipid-lowering indication — 10–20 g/day total psyllium husk, typically divided into two or three doses with meals, incorporated into food or taken as a suspension; minimum 4–8 week treatment period required to assess lipid response. Dosage forms: oral powder/granules for suspension; capsules (as husk); wafers; psyllium-enriched food products. Paediatric dosing (>6 years): half the adult dose with proportionally increased fluid; not recommended in children under 6 years without medical supervision. Critical administration instruction: always take with a full glass of water; inadequate fluid intake risks esophageal or intestinal obstruction.
results.condition: 1. Chronic idiopathic constipation (bulk-forming laxative, first-line non-pharmacological agent). 2. Irritable bowel syndrome with constipation [IBS-C] (reduces stool hardness and improves bowel frequency). 3. Hypercholesterolaemia / dyslipidaemia (adjunctive LDL-cholesterol lowering; FDA-approved health claim for cardiovascular disease risk reduction). 4. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — adjunctive management of postprandial hyperglycaemia. 5. Diarrhoea — symptomatic normalisation of stool consistency (traditional and pharmacopoeial use). 6. Haemorrhoids — softening stool to reduce straining (traditional use, supported by guidelines).

results.original_text: Fructus Serenoae Repentis — Serenoa repens (W. Bartram) Small (Arecaceae). Dried ripe fruit. Key chemical constituents: free fatty acids (lauric, oleic, myristic, palmitic, linoleic, capric acids; collectively comprising the majority of the lipidosterolic extract); phytosterols (beta-sitosterol, campesterol, stigmasterol, cycloartenol); flavonoids (isoquercitrin, kaempferol); polysaccharides; and high-molecular-weight alcohols (including aliphatic alcohols). Lipidosterolic extract (lipid/ethanol or hexane extract) standardised to contain 80–90% total fatty acids and sterols.
results.translation: Saw palmetto (Serenoa repens) berry extract — particularly its lipidosterolic [lipid and sterol-containing] fraction — is primarily used to relieve lower urinary tract symptoms associated with benign prostatic hyperplasia [non-cancerous enlargement of the prostate gland, abbreviated BPH], including urinary frequency, nocturia [waking at night to urinate], weak stream, and incomplete bladder emptying. Its pharmacological activity is attributed to multiple mechanisms: inhibition of both isoforms of 5-alpha-reductase (the enzyme that converts testosterone to the more potent dihydrotestosterone, which drives prostate cell proliferation); anti-androgenic and anti-oestrogenic receptor-binding activity; inhibition of inflammatory mediators (cyclooxygenase and 5-lipoxygenase pathways); and possible antiproliferative effects on prostatic epithelial and stromal cells. Several double-blind, placebo-controlled clinical trials and meta-analyses have demonstrated statistically significant improvements in urinary flow rate and symptom scores (e.g., International Prostate Symptom Score [IPSS]) compared to placebo, with efficacy broadly comparable to the pharmaceutical drug finasteride in some comparative trials, but with a more favourable adverse-effect profile. It is also recorded in traditional medicine for treatment of irritable bladder, chronic pelvic pain, and historically as a nutritive tonic and mild aphrodisiac.
results.preparation: Oral lipidosterolic (hexane or ethanol) extract standardised to 80–90% fatty acids and sterols: 160 mg twice daily (320 mg/day total), or a single daily dose of 320 mg. Crude dried berry preparations: 1–2 g of ground dried fruit per day, taken as decoction or incorporated into capsules/tablets. Tincture (1:4, 70% ethanol): 2–4 mL three times daily. WHO recommended adult dose (clinical evidence-supported): lipidosterolic extract 320 mg/day in divided doses. Treatment duration in clinical trials ranged from 4 weeks to 3 years; effects are typically assessed after a minimum of 4–6 weeks of continuous use.
results.condition: Benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) — lower urinary tract symptoms; urinary frequency and nocturia; irritable bladder (traditional use)
results.source_section: WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 1 — Fructus Serenoae Repentis

entities_mentioned.name: Serenoa repens
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Saw palmetto — Serenoa repens (W. Bartram) Small, family Arecaceae (palm family); synonym Sabal serrulata (Michx.) Schult. & Schult.f.
entities_mentioned.notes: Small, shrubby fan palm native to the southeastern United States (coastal plains from South Carolina to Florida and west to Louisiana). Fruits are blue-black drupes harvested in autumn. The plant is the sole species in genus Serenoa.

entities_mentioned.name: Fructus Serenoae Repentis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pharmaceutical Latin designation for the dried ripe fruit of Serenoa repens
entities_mentioned.notes: Pharmacopoeial name used in the WHO monograph and European Pharmacopoeia (Ph. Eur.). The lipidosterolic extract (LSESr) is the commercially and clinically predominant preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: beta-sitosterol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Phytosterol (plant sterol); CAS 83-46-5
entities_mentioned.notes: A primary phytosterol constituent of saw palmetto extract also studied independently for BPH symptom relief.

entities_mentioned.name: 5-alpha-reductase
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Steroid 5-alpha-reductase (EC 1.3.1.22); two isoforms (type I and type II) catalyse the conversion of testosterone to dihydrotestosterone (DHT)
entities_mentioned.notes: Inhibition of this enzyme is the primary proposed mechanism of action for BPH treatment by saw palmetto extract and synthetic drugs such as finasteride.

entities_mentioned.name: finasteride
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Synthetic 5-alpha-reductase inhibitor (type II selective); pharmaceutical comparator drug used in clinical trials against saw palmetto
entities_mentioned.notes: Saw palmetto extract showed comparable IPSS score improvements to finasteride in one major comparative RCT (Carraro et al., 1996) with fewer adverse effects (particularly regarding sexual function).

results.original_text: Herba Tanaceti Parthenii (Tanacetum parthenium). Key chemical constituents: sesquiterpene lactones (principally parthenolide, ≥0.2% in dried herb per some pharmacopoeial standards), flavonoids (apigenin, luteolin, chrysoeriol, quercetin), volatile oils (camphor, camphene, p-cymene, terpinene, bornyl acetate), polyacetylenes, and melatonin. Parthenolide is considered the principal bioactive marker compound.
results.translation: Feverfew (Tanacetum parthenium) is a perennial herb in the Asteraceae family, native to southeastern Europe and western Asia but now widely naturalized and cultivated globally. The aerial parts — leaves, stems, and flowers — collected during or just before flowering constitute the medicinal drug [Herba Tanaceti Parthenii]. Its primary evidence-based clinical application is the prophylactic (preventive) treatment of migraine headaches; it is not regarded as effective for acute migraine relief. The principal active constituent, parthenolide (a sesquiterpene lactone), inhibits platelet aggregation, suppresses the release of serotonin [5-hydroxytryptamine] from platelets and polymorphonuclear leucocytes, and inhibits prostaglandin synthesis and arachidonic acid metabolism — mechanisms collectively thought to interrupt the cascade of vascular and neurochemical events underlying migraine. In vitro and in vivo studies further demonstrate anti-inflammatory activity via inhibition of NF-κB signalling and phospholipase A2. Multiple randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled trials (including a landmark 1988 trial by Murphy et al. in the Lancet, and a 1988 Johnson et al. crossover trial) demonstrated statistically significant reductions in the frequency and severity of migraine attacks in patients taking daily feverfew, though effect sizes were moderate. A 1996 Palevitch et al. trial similarly found significant reductions in pain intensity and associated symptoms (nausea, vomiting, sensitivity to light and noise). Adverse reactions include mouth ulceration and inflammation of the oral mucosa (particularly with fresh leaf chewing), gastrointestinal upset, and a post-feverfew syndrome (rebound headache, anxiety, insomnia, muscle stiffness) upon abrupt discontinuation after prolonged use. Feverfew may potentiate anticoagulant and antiplatelet drugs [e.g., warfarin, aspirin] and should not be used concurrently. It is contraindicated in pregnancy (due to potential uterotonic activity), in patients with known hypersensitivity to members of the Asteraceae/Compositae family, and in children under 12 years. The WHO-supported adult dosage for migraine prophylaxis is 125 mg/day of a dried leaf preparation standardised to contain at least 0.2% parthenolide, taken as a single daily dose; alternatively, 0.25–0.5 mg of parthenolide per day is the target intake. Tincture preparations (1:5 in 25% alcohol) at 5–20 drops daily have also been described in traditional use.

entities_mentioned.name: Tanacetum parthenium
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Feverfew — Asteraceae family; synonyms include Chrysanthemum parthenium (L.) Bernh. and Pyrethrum parthenium (L.) Sm.
entities_mentioned.notes: Formerly classified under Chrysanthemum and Pyrethrum genera. The WHO monograph accepts Tanacetum parthenium as the current accepted binomial.

entities_mentioned.name: Parthenolide
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sesquiterpene lactone; CAS 20554-84-1; principal bioactive and pharmacopoeial marker compound of Herba Tanaceti Parthenii
entities_mentioned.notes: Parthenolide content varies significantly with cultivation conditions, geographic origin, plant part, and post-harvest processing. Minimum 0.2% is cited as the quality threshold in several European standards.

entities_mentioned.name: Herba Tanaceti Parthenii
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pharmaceutical Latin name for the dried aerial parts of Tanacetum parthenium
entities_mentioned.notes: The drug consists of the dried leaves, stems, and flower heads harvested at or near flowering. Some preparations use the dried leaf alone.

entities_mentioned.name: Migraine prophylaxis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Preventive treatment of recurrent migraine headache
entities_mentioned.notes: This is the sole use supported by multiple clinical trials. Feverfew is not indicated for acute migraine treatment.

entities_mentioned.name: Murphy et al. (1988)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Randomised double-blind placebo-controlled crossover trial published in The Lancet; found significant reduction in migraine frequency and severity with feverfew 70–86 mg/day dried leaf
entities_mentioned.notes: Landmark trial frequently cited in WHO and ESCOP monographs as primary evidence for feverfew efficacy.

entities_mentioned.name: NF-κB
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nuclear factor kappa-light-chain-enhancer of activated B cells — a key transcription factor in inflammatory signalling
entities_mentioned.notes: Parthenolide inhibits NF-κB by alkylating cysteine residues in the IκB kinase complex, providing a molecular basis for anti-inflammatory activity.
